tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: ADT with Docetaxel and Abiraterone (Triplet Therapy)

drugs.name: Androgen deprivation therapy (LHRH agonist/antagonist/orchiectomy)
drugs.schedule: Continuous to maintain castrate levels (<50 ng/dL)

tier_1_regimens.notes: PEACE-1 was a 2×2 factorial design. Grade ≥3 AEs occurred in 63% of triplet vs 52% of ADT+docetaxel. Most common grade ≥3 AEs with triplet: neutropenia, febrile neutropenia, fatigue, neuropathy. Intended for high-volume mCSPC patients fit for chemotherapy [556].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: ADT with Docetaxel and Darolutamide (Triplet Therapy)

drugs.name: Androgen deprivation therapy (LHRH agonist/antagonist/orchiectomy)
drugs.schedule: Continuous to maintain castrate levels (<50 ng/dL)

tier_1_regimens.notes: ARASENS showed improved OS at 4 years: 62.7% vs 50.4%. Also improved time to CRPC (HR 0.36), skeletal event-free survival (HR 0.61), and time to subsequent therapy (HR 0.39). Adverse events of any grade and grade 3-5 were similar between arms. Rash (16.6% vs 13.5%) and hypertension (13.7% vs 9.2%) were more frequent in darolutamide arm [557,559].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: ADT with Docetaxel and Enzalutamide (Triplet Therapy)

drugs.name: Androgen deprivation therapy (LHRH agonist/antagonist/orchiectomy)
drugs.schedule: Continuous to maintain castrate levels (<50 ng/dL)

tier_1_regimens.notes: ENZAMET compared enzalutamide+ADT to first-generation antiandrogen+ADT with concurrent docetaxel allowed. OS improved regardless of docetaxel use. Most common grade ≥3 AEs: febrile neutropenia (6%), fatigue (6%), hypertension (10%) [546,547].

tier_1_regimens.site: Low-Volume M1 CSPC, High-Volume M1 CSPC

drugs.name: Androgen deprivation therapy (LHRH agonist/antagonist/orchiectomy)
drugs.schedule: Continuous to maintain castrate levels (<50 ng/dL)

drugs.name: Prednisone
drugs.schedule: Once daily (without docetaxel); Twice daily (with docetaxel)

tier_1_regimens.notes: LATITUDE: 5-year OS 52.5% vs 34.5%. STAMPEDE: OS benefit in M1, N1, and M0 subgroups. Alternative fine-particle formulation at 500 mg with methylprednisolone 4 mg PO BID is category 2B. Low-dose abiraterone 250 mg/day with low-fat breakfast also an option for patients who cannot tolerate or afford standard dose [538,539,541,542].

tier_1_regimens.site: Low-Volume M1 CSPC, High-Volume M1 CSPC

drugs.name: Androgen deprivation therapy (LHRH agonist/antagonist/orchiectomy)
drugs.schedule: Continuous to maintain castrate levels (<50 ng/dL)

tier_1_regimens.notes: TITAN: 44-month median follow-up, OS not reached vs 52.2 months. More common AEs: rash, hypothyroidism, ischemic heart disease. HRQOL maintained during treatment [543,545].

tier_1_regimens.site: Low-Volume M1 CSPC, High-Volume M1 CSPC

drugs.name: Androgen deprivation therapy (LHRH agonist/antagonist/orchiectomy)
drugs.schedule: Continuous to maintain castrate levels (<50 ng/dL)

tier_1_regimens.notes: ENZAMET: OS benefit maintained at 68-month follow-up. ARCHES: OS benefit with 34% risk reduction, may be underestimated due to 32% crossover to enzalutamide. AEs include fatigue, seizures, hypertension [546-549].

tier_1_regimens.site: Low-Volume M1 CSPC, High-Volume M1 CSPC

drugs.name: Androgen deprivation therapy (LHRH agonist/antagonist/orchiectomy)
drugs.schedule: Continuous to maintain castrate levels (<50 ng/dL)

tier_1_regimens.notes: ARANOTE: rPFS improved but OS not yet significant. FDA approved June 2025. Not category 1 because OS benefit not demonstrated for doublet therapy. Consistent benefit across low- and high-volume subgroups [550].

tier_1_regimens.site: Low-Volume M1 CSPC, High-Volume M1 CSPC

drugs.name: Androgen deprivation therapy (LHRH agonist/antagonist/orchiectomy)
drugs.schedule: Continuous to maintain castrate levels (<50 ng/dL)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Most patients (78%) had high-volume and/or synchronous disease. Grade 3/4 AEs more common with niraparib arm (75% vs 59%). Most common grade 3/4 AEs: anemia (29.1% vs 4.6%), hypertension (26.5% vs 18.4%). Treatment-emergent AEs leading to death: 14 vs 7 participants. OS data immature. Can be used following docetaxel completion in triplet therapy or after EBRT to primary tumor. Benefit in low-volume disease is controversial [560].

drugs.name: Androgen deprivation therapy (LHRH agonist/antagonist/orchiectomy)
drugs.schedule: Continuous to maintain castrate levels (<50 ng/dL)

tier_1_regimens.notes: CHAARTED: Median OS 57.6 vs 47.2 months. Subgroup analysis showed OS benefit predominantly in high-volume disease (HR 0.63). Low-volume disease did not show definitive survival benefit (HR 1.04). STAMPEDE confirmed benefit. Panel notes triplets of ADT+docetaxel+ARPI show improved OS over ADT+docetaxel, so docetaxel alone is not included as preferred option but may be considered [551-553].

drugs.name: Leuprolide
drugs.schedule: Per standard ADT schedule (optional, with or without)

biomarkers.requirement: PSADT ≤9 months; PSA ≥2 ng/mL above nadir after RT or ≥1 ng/mL after RP ± post-op RT; not candidate for pelvic-directed therapy

tier_1_regimens.notes: EMBARK enrolled patients with high-risk BCR: M0 by CT/MRI/bone scan, PSADT ≤9 months. Median follow-up 60.7 months. Common AEs: hot flashes, fatigue. Enzalutamide monotherapy associated with gynecomastia (45%), nipple pain (15%), breast tenderness (14%) [478].

tier_1_regimens.setting: High-risk BCR2 (post-RP, received prior adjuvant or secondary RT or not candidate for RT)

biomarkers.requirement: PSADT ≤9 months; PSA ≥0.5 ng/mL; prior adjuvant or secondary RT or not candidate for RT

tier_1_regimens.notes: PRESTO: Median PSA-PFS 24.9 months (ADT+apalutamide) vs 20.3 months (ADT alone). Planned treatment duration 52 weeks. Common AEs: hot flashes (78%), fatigue (55%), hypertension (31% any grade, 7-8% grade ≥3 in arms A and B) [479].

COU-AA-302: Asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic mCRPC, no visceral metastases, no prior ketoconazole. PSA decline ≥50%: 62% vs 24%. Radiographic responses: 36% vs 16%. Fine-particle formulation 500 mg with methylprednisolone 4 mg BID also available [583,584].
PREVAIL: Chemotherapy-naive mCRPC. No food restrictions. Concurrent prednisone permitted but not required. Seizure risk 0.9% per FDA label [593,594].

drugs.name: Prednisone
drugs.schedule: Daily (may omit on chemo day if dexamethasone given)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Every-3-week schedule is standard. Alternative: 50 mg/m2 every 2 weeks (phase 2 data showed similar OS with less toxicity). ≥8 cycles may be associated with better OS. Duration based on benefit and toxicities assessment [607-611].

drugs.name: Prednisone
drugs.schedule: Daily (may omit on chemo day if dexamethasone given)

tier_1_regimens.notes: TROPIC: 25 mg/m2 OS benefit vs mitoxantrone. PROSELICA: 20 mg/m2 noninferior to 25 mg/m2 for OS with less toxicity. 20 mg/m2 is recommended for fit patients; 25 mg/m2 may be considered for healthy patients. Biweekly 16 mg/m2 with G-CSF an option for patients ≥65 years (CABASTY trial). Grade ≥3 AEs: febrile neutropenia, diarrhea, fatigue [613-620].

biomarkers.requirement: Visceral metastases, low PSA and bulky disease, high LDH, high CEA, lytic bone metastases, NEPC histology, or unfavorable genomics (defects in ≥2 of PTEN, TP53, RB1)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Post-hoc analysis showed patients with aggressive variant disease had longer median PFS with combination (7.5 vs 1.7 months; P = .017). Most common grade 3-5 AEs: fatigue, anemia, neutropenia, thrombocytopenia — all more common in the combination arm [622].

biomarkers.requirement: No definitive evidence of progression on prior docetaxel in castration-sensitive or mCRPC setting

tier_1_regimens.notes: Retrospective data from GETUG-AFU 15 suggests docetaxel may benefit CRPC patients who received docetaxel in the CSPC setting without definitive progression. Not recommended for patients who received docetaxel in CSPC setting without prior ARPI and then present with pre-ARPI mCRPC [611,612].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Post-ARPI (any prior docetaxel), BRCAm or HRRm

biomarkers.requirement: BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, BARD1, BRIP1, CDK12, CHEK1, CHEK2, FANCL, PALB2, RAD51B, RAD51C, RAD51D, or RAD54L mutation (germline and/or somatic)

tier_1_regimens.notes: PROfound: Efficacy driven by BRCA2/BRCA1/ATM cohort. OS benefit maintained despite 66% crossover in control arm. Adverse events: anemia (including requiring transfusion), fatigue, nausea/vomiting, thrombocytopenia, creatinine elevation. Theoretical risk of myelodysplasia/AML [652,653].

biomarkers.requirement: Pathogenic BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation (germline and/or somatic)

tier_1_regimens.notes: TRITON2: BRCA1/2-mutated mCRPC post-ARPI and taxane. TRITON3: rPFS improved with rucaparib vs physician's choice. Should not be used in patients with HRR mutations other than BRCA1/2. Adverse events: anemia, fatigue, nausea [654-656].

biomarkers.requirement: Pathogenic BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation (germline and/or somatic)

tier_1_regimens.notes: PROpel: rPFS 24.8 vs 16.6 months overall. Final OS not significant in full cohort (P = .054). Post-hoc OS benefit in BRCAm: HR 0.29 (95% CI, 0.14-0.56). No OS benefit in non-HRRm or non-BRCAm/other HRRm subgroups [661,662].

biomarkers.requirement: BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, ATR, CDK12, CHEK2, FANCA, MLH1, MRE11A, NBN, PALB2, or RAD51C mutation (germline and/or somatic)

tier_1_regimens.notes: TALAPRO-2: HRRm cohort OS 45.1 vs 31.1 months. BRCAm had 77% lower risk of radiographic progression (HR 0.23). Grade 3-4 AEs: anemia (31%), thrombocytopenia (9%), neutropenia (8%). Dose interruption due to AEs in 75% of talazoparib group vs 23% placebo [664,665].

biomarkers.requirement: Pathogenic BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutation (germline and/or somatic)

tier_1_regimens.notes: MAGNITUDE: HRRm group rPFS 16.5 vs 13.7 months. Non-BRCA HRRm subgroup showed no benefit. Non-HRRm cohort: futility declared. Second interim analysis suggested possible OS benefit (HR 0.54; 95% CI, 0.33-0.90; P = .0181). Grade ≥3 AEs: anemia 28.3% vs 7.6%, hypertension 14.6% vs 12.3% [666,667].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Pre-ARPI (after progression on one ARPI in M0 CRPC), BRCA1/2m
tier_1_regimens.notes: TRITON3: Post-ARPI, no prior chemotherapy for mCRPC. Primary endpoint met. Should not be used in patients with HRR gene mutations other than BRCA1/2 [656].

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: Olaparib plus Abiraterone (Pre-ARPI mCRPC — without specific BRCA requirement)

biomarkers.marker: None specific (but benefit primarily in BRCAm/HRRm)
biomarkers.requirement: Untreated mCRPC (PROpel enrollment); benefit strongest in BRCAm

tier_1_regimens.notes: FDA approved for BRCAm mCRPC. While PROpel enrolled ITT population without HRR requirement, the Panel includes this as category 1 for BRCAm mCRPC. Fine-particle abiraterone may be substituted for standard formulation [661,662].

Use of this combination for patients who have received prior ARPI without prior docetaxel is controversial (category 2B) because a benefit of this combination over use of a PARP inhibitor alone has not been shown in this setting [666,667].

biomarkers.requirement: BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, ATR, CDK12, CHEK2, FANCA, MLH1, MRE11A, NBN, PALB2, or RAD51C mutation

tier_1_regimens.notes: Use of talazoparib/enzalutamide for patients who have received prior ARPI without prior docetaxel is controversial (category 2B) because a benefit of this combination over a PARP inhibitor alone has not been shown in this setting, but responses are likely [664,665].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Any prior therapy (useful in certain circumstances)

biomarkers.requirement: Microsatellite instability-high or mismatch repair deficiency

tier_1_regimens.notes: Prevalence of MMR deficiency in mCRPC is 2%-5%. If MSI-H/dMMR found, refer to genetic counseling for Lynch syndrome assessment. Limited data suggest benefit in TMB ≥10 mut/mB mCRPC (Lenis AT, et al. Clin Canc Res 2024;30:3894-3903). May cause severe immune-mediated adverse reactions [592,625-631].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Any prior therapy (asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic)

drugs.name: Sipuleucel-T
drugs.schedule: Leukapheresis, then 3 infusions 2 weeks apart (at weeks 0, 2, 4)

biomarkers.requirement: Asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic; no liver metastases; ECOG 0-1; life expectancy >6 months

tier_1_regimens.notes: 22% mortality risk reduction. 18.2% had prior chemotherapy. Not recommended for small cell prostate cancer/NEPC. Benefit has not been reported in patients with visceral metastases. Limited data to support efficacy post-chemotherapy. Common complications: chills (54.1%), pyrexia (29.3%), headache (16.0%) [623,624].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Post-ARPI, post-docetaxel or pre-docetaxel (delay taxane); PSMA-positive

biomarkers.requirement: ≥1 PSMA-positive lesion; no dominant PSMA-negative metastatic lesions (defined as bone with soft tissue ≥1.0 cm, lymph nodes ≥2.5 cm short axis, solid organ ≥1.0 cm)

tier_1_regimens.notes: VISION: Post-ARPI and post-taxane setting. PSMAfore: Pre-taxane, post-ARPI. OS not significantly different in PSMAfore due to crossover (57% of control arm received Lu-177-PSMA-617). Grade ≥3 AEs (VISION): anemia, thrombocytopenia, lymphopenia, fatigue — more common with Lu-177-PSMA-617. PSMA imaging should be used to determine eligibility [668,669].

tier_1_regimens.setting: Symptomatic bone metastases, no visceral metastases

biomarkers.requirement: Symptomatic bone metastases; no visceral metastatic disease

tier_1_regimens.notes: 57% received prior docetaxel. Extends time to first SRE (median 15.6 vs 9.8 months). Grade 3/4 hematologic toxicity low: neutropenia 3%, thrombocytopenia 6%, anemia 13%. Should not be used in patients with visceral metastases. Concurrent use with enzalutamide may be considered (PEACE-3 trial). Concomitant denosumab or zoledronic acid recommended. Increased fracture risk when combined with ARPI without bone agent [670-676].

PEACE-3: rPFS 16.4 vs 19.4 months, OS 35.0 vs 42.3 months. Grade ≥3 AEs: 65.6% vs 55.8%. Fractures: 24.3% vs 13.4% (without bone agents fracture rate was 45.9% vs 22.3%). Mandatory concomitant bone-protecting agent use [675,676].

trial_data.SRE_delay: Median 20.7 vs 17.1 months (HR, 0.82; P = .008 for superiority)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Preferred over zoledronic acid based on longer time to first SRE. Similar spinal cord compression, need for RT, and pathologic fracture rates. Hypocalcemia more common with denosumab (13% vs 6%). ONJ 1%-2%. Supplemental calcium and vitamin D recommended [684,687].

biomarkers.requirement: CRPC with bone metastases; creatinine clearance ≥30 mL/min

tier_1_regimens.notes: Every-12-week dosing noninferior to every-4-week dosing (Himelstein et al. JAMA 2017). Dose reduction for CrCl 30-60 mL/min; hold for CrCl <30 mL/min. Not recommended in CSPC bone metastases [684,685,688,689].
Treatment for small cell/neuroendocrine prostate cancer is extrapolated from NCCN Guidelines for Small Cell Lung Cancer. Refer to NCCN SCLC guidelines for additional options [679-683].
Extrapolated from NCCN SCLC guidelines. Carboplatin is an alternative to cisplatin [679-683].
Refer to NCCN SCLC guidelines [679-683].

biomarkers.requirement: Small cell/neuroendocrine prostate cancer or aggressive variant mCRPC

tier_1_regimens.notes: Can be considered for aggressive variant mCRPC or NEPC [622].

drugs.name: Stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT)
drugs.dose: Per MDT dose guidelines (e.g., 30-35 Gy in 5 fractions)

biomarkers.requirement: Oligometastatic disease (≤5 by CT/MRI/bone scan or ≤10 by PSMA-PET)

tier_1_regimens.guideline_category: Preferred (metachronous oligorecurrent); Useful in certain circumstances (synchronous)
tier_1_regimens.notes: ORIOLE/STOMP combined analysis: pooled HR 0.44 (P < .001) for PFS at 52.5-month follow-up. EXTEND: PFS improved with MDT vs intermittent ADT alone. ARTO (mCRPC): PFS improved with MDRT+abiraterone (HR 0.35; P < .001). MDT may also use lymph node dissection in select cases [565-570,677].

biomarkers.requirement: Symptomatic patients unable to tolerate other therapies

tier_1_regimens.notes: No survival benefit demonstrated in two randomized trials. May provide palliation for pain. Adverse events similar to docetaxel but lower rates of grade 3-4 neutropenic fevers [633,634].

biomarkers.requirement: Elevated fracture risk based on FRAX; 10-year risk of hip fracture ≥3% or major osteoporotic fracture ≥20%

tier_1_regimens.notes: Recommended when absolute fracture risk warrants drug therapy. Baseline DEXA before ADT; follow-up DEXA after 1 year of therapy. Hypocalcemia risk factors: blastic bone metastases, renal impairment, vitamin D deficiency. Do not stop without bisphosphonate coverage due to rebound bone loss [528,532].
Alternative to denosumab and zoledronic acid for treatment-related bone loss during ADT [531].
Alternative to denosumab and alendronate for treatment-related bone loss during ADT. Monitor renal function [529,530].

tier_2_handbook.definition: Prostate cancer is a malignancy arising from the epithelial cells of the prostate gland, an exocrine organ of the male reproductive system located posterior to the pubic symphysis and anterior to the rectum. The prostate is embryologically derived from the urogenital sinus, specifically from the endodermal cloaca. Its anatomical boundaries are defined by the bladder neck superiorly, the urogenital diaphragm inferiorly, the seminal vesicles and vas deferens posteriorly, and the rectoprostatic fascia (Denonvilliers' fascia) separating it from the rectum. The prostate is divided into distinct zones: the peripheral zone (where ~70% of adenocarcinomas arise), the central zone, and the transition zone (common site for benign prostatic hyperplasia). The most common histologic type is adenocarcinoma, which typically originates in the peripheral zone. Clinical suspicion arises from an abnormal digital rectal exam (DRE) or an elevated prostate-specific antigen (PSA) level. Definitive diagnosis requires histologic confirmation via prostate biopsy, usually performed under transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) guidance. The pathologic grade is determined using the Gleason grading system, which has been updated to a 5-tier Grade Group system (1-5) based on the Gleason score [1, 2]. Clinical staging follows the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) TNM system (8th edition), incorporating clinical stage (cT), pathologic stage (pT), lymph node status (N), and metastasis status (M) [3]. Risk stratification for treatment purposes is based on these clinical and pathologic features, including TNM stage, Grade Group, and PSA level, which are used to assign patients to NCCN risk groups (low, favorable intermediate, unfavorable intermediate, high, and very high) [4].

subtypes.name: Adenocarcinoma
subtypes.description: The most common histologic subtype (~95% of cases), typically arising from glandular epithelial cells of the prostate. It is graded using the Gleason system (Grade Groups 1-5) based on architectural patterns.
subtypes.key_associations: Strong association with androgen receptor signaling. Molecular alterations often include TMPRSS2-ERG gene fusion, PTEN loss, and mutations in genes like SPOP, FOXA1, and TP53.

subtypes.name: Ductal Adenocarcinoma
subtypes.description: A rare variant (approximately 1.3% of prostate carcinomas) characterized by growth patterns resembling the ducts of the normal prostate. It may exhibit papillary, cribriform, or flat growth patterns.
subtypes.key_associations: Often diagnosed at a more advanced stage. May be associated with intraductal carcinoma and cribriform architecture, which are features linked to poorer prognosis.

subtypes.name: Intraductal Carcinoma of the Prostate (IDC-P)
subtypes.description: A histologic pattern defined by cribriform proliferation of malignant cells confined within preexisting prostatic ducts surrounded by a layer of basal cells. It is frequently associated with invasive carcinoma.
subtypes.frequency: More common in higher risk groups.
subtypes.key_associations: Strongly associated with high-grade invasive adenocarcinoma, genomic instability, and poor prognosis. Often harbors genomic alterations associated with aggressive disease.

subtypes.name: Neuroendocrine Prostate Cancer (NEPC) / Small Cell Carcinoma
subtypes.description: A rare and aggressive variant that may arise de novo or, more commonly, as a treatment-emergent differentiation following androgen deprivation therapy. Histology shows small, round, blue cells with neuroendocrine features.
subtypes.frequency: De novo is rare; treatment-emergent variant seen in ~17% of mCRPC patients at autopsy.
subtypes.key_associations: Associated with aggressive clinical behavior, visceral metastases, low PSA levels relative to tumor burden, and poor response to hormonal therapies. May harbor RB1, TP53 alterations.

subtypes.name: Squamous Cell Carcinoma
subtypes.description: A very rare non-adenocarcinoma histologic type characterized by malignant squamous differentiation.
subtypes.key_associations: Does not produce PSA. Often presents with local symptoms and has an aggressive course.

subtypes.name: Transitional Cell (Urothelial) Carcinoma
subtypes.description: Arises from the urothelium lining the prostatic urethra and ducts. More common in the bladder than in the prostate.
subtypes.key_associations: May coexist with bladder cancer. Treatment often aligns with urothelial carcinoma protocols.

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: Prostate cancer pathogenesis is driven by a complex interplay of genetic, epigenetic, and microenvironmental factors. A hallmark early event is the androgen receptor (AR) signaling pathway, which is critical for tumor growth and survival. Key genomic events include: 1) **TMPRSS2-ERG gene fusion** (present in ~50% of cases), which places the ERG oncogene under the control of the androgen-responsive TMPRSS2 promoter, leading to aberrant ERG expression [5]. 2) **PTEN loss** (via deletion or mutation), occurring in ~40-50% of metastatic cases, leads to activation of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway and is associated with aggressive disease [5]. 3) **TP53 and RB1 inactivation** (more frequent in advanced/metastatic disease), contributing to genomic instability and loss of cell cycle control [5]. 4) **AR amplification, mutations, or splicing variants** (e.g., AR-V7) drive progression to castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC) by maintaining AR signaling despite androgen deprivation [5]. 5) **Somatic mutations in DNA repair genes**, most notably **BRCA1/BRCA2** (germline or somatic), are found in up to 23% of mCRPC tumors and confer sensitivity to PARP inhibitors and platinum chemotherapy [6, 7]. Other frequently mutated genes include ATM, PALB2, FANCA, RAD51D, CHEK2, and CDK12 [6, 7]. 6) **Defects in DNA mismatch repair (MMR) genes** (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) lead to microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H)/deficient MMR (dMMR) status in a subset of tumors (estimated 2-5%), making them potentially responsive to immune checkpoint inhibitors like pembrolizumab [8]. 7) **SPOP mutations** are the most common point mutation in primary prostate cancer. 8) **MYC amplification and TP53/RB1 loss** are associated with neuroendocrine prostate cancer (NEPC) differentiation, a treatment-emergent aggressive phenotype [5]. Chromosomal abnormalities like deletions of 8p (harboring NKX3.1) and gains of 8q (harboring MYC) are common. Epigenetic alterations, including DNA hypermethylation, also play a role in silencing tumor suppressor genes [5].

epidemiology.incidence: Prostate cancer is the most common non-cutaneous cancer diagnosed in men in the United States. An estimated 313,780 new cases were projected for 2025, accounting for 30% of all new cancer diagnoses in men [1]. The lifetime risk of developing prostate cancer is 1 in 8 for men in the U.S. [1].
epidemiology.mortality: An estimated 35,770 deaths from prostate cancer were projected for 2025, making it the second leading cause of cancer death in men in the U.S., accounting for 11% of male cancer deaths [1]. The 5-year relative survival rate for all stages combined is 97% [1].
epidemiology.trends: Prostate cancer incidence declined by approximately 40% from 2007 to 2014, largely reflecting changes in PSA screening recommendations following the 2012 USPSTF recommendation against routine screening. Since 2014, incidence has been increasing at a rate of ~3% annually [1, 9]. This trend is associated with a rise in diagnoses of regional and metastatic disease [1, 9]. The age-adjusted death rate declined by 52% from 1993 to 2017, but has stabilized in recent years with a 0.5% annual decrease from 2012 through 2022 [1].
epidemiology.demographics: Risk increases with age; it is rarely diagnosed before age 50. There are profound racial and ethnic disparities. The incidence rate in Black individuals is 67% higher than in White individuals, and they have a 1 in 6 lifetime risk [10, 11]. Black individuals are more likely to be diagnosed with more aggressive disease at a younger age and are less likely to have had a PSA test within the past year [11]. The mortality rate in Black men is two to four times higher than in all other racial/ethnic groups, accounting for 17% of male cancer deaths in the U.S. [1, 11]. Hispanic patients are more likely than non-Hispanic White patients to present with higher-risk localized or metastatic disease [12, 13]. Asian American, Native Hawaiian, and Pacific Islander individuals are more likely to be diagnosed at a higher risk group than White individuals [14]. Data show that when patients receive guideline-concordant care, overall prognosis by race appears similar [15].

risk_factors.detail: Risk increases dramatically with age. Prostate cancer is rare before age 50, and the majority of cases are diagnosed in men over 65.

Black men have a 67% higher incidence and significantly higher mortality compared to White men. The reasons are multifactorial, likely involving a combination of genetic, biological, social, and healthcare access factors [11].

A first-degree relative with prostate cancer increases risk 2- to 3-fold. The risk is higher with multiple affected relatives or a relative diagnosed at a young age (<60 years) [16]. Specific hereditary cancer syndromes, such as Hereditary Breast and Ovarian Cancer (HBOC) syndrome (due to germline BRCA1/2 mutations) and Lynch syndrome, significantly elevate risk [6].

Pathogenic germline mutations in homologous recombination repair (HRR) genes, particularly BRCA2, are associated with a 2- to 6-fold increased risk of prostate cancer and more aggressive disease. Mutations in ATM, PALB2, and CHEK2 also increase risk [6, 17].

Incidence is highest in North America, Northwestern Europe, Australia, and the Caribbean. It is lower in Asia and Africa, though migrating populations from low-risk areas gradually adopt the higher risk of their new region, suggesting lifestyle and environmental factors.

High consumption of red meat and high-fat dairy products, and low consumption of fruits and vegetables, may be associated with a slightly increased risk. Obesity (high BMI) is linked to a higher risk of aggressive prostate cancer.
Higher endogenous androgen levels or androgen receptor activity are thought to promote prostate cancer development.
Some studies suggest a possible association with an increased risk, but evidence is not consistent.
Widespread use of PSA testing leads to increased detection of prostate cancer (overdiagnosis), including many indolent tumors that might not have caused symptoms or death during a patient's lifetime.

protective_factors: 5-alpha reductase inhibitors (e.g., finasteride, dutasteride) have been shown in large randomized trials to reduce the risk of low-grade prostate cancer by about 25%, but the absolute risk reduction and potential for diagnosing higher-grade disease need careful consideration [18].
Regular vigorous exercise may be associated with a modestly reduced risk of aggressive prostate cancer, though evidence is not conclusive.

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Prostate cancer is often diagnosed following an abnormal digital rectal examination (DRE) or an elevated prostate-specific antigen (PSA) level, frequently discovered during screening or routine evaluation. The initial suspicion of prostate cancer is based on these findings. A definitive diagnosis requires biopsies of the prostate, usually performed by a urologist using a needle under transrectal ultrasound (TRUS) guidance. A pathologist assigns a Gleason primary and secondary grade to the biopsy specimen. Many patients are asymptomatic at the time of diagnosis, particularly those detected through PSA-based screening. Prostate cancer is the most common cancer in men in the United States, with an estimated 313,780 new cases diagnosed in 2025, accounting for 30% of new cancer cases in men. The lifetime risk of developing prostate cancer is 1 in 8. For all stages combined, the 5-year relative survival rate for prostate cancer is 97%. When symptoms do occur, they may include urinary symptoms (difficulty starting or stopping urination, weak or interrupted flow, frequent urination especially at night, difficulty emptying the bladder completely, and burning or pain during urination), bone pain, unexplained weight loss, and fatigue. These symptoms are more common in advanced disease.

symptoms.name: Urinary obstruction symptoms
symptoms.detail: Difficulty starting urination, weak or interrupted urinary stream, frequent urination especially at night (nocturia), difficulty emptying the bladder completely. These may result from local prostate enlargement.
symptoms.frequency: Common in locally advanced disease; less common in early-stage disease detected through screening

symptoms.name: Bone pain
symptoms.detail: Pain in the back, hips, ribs, or other bones may indicate osseous metastatic disease. Bone metastases are a common site of spread.

symptoms.name: Unexplained weight loss
symptoms.detail: Significant unexplained weight loss may indicate advanced or metastatic disease.
symptoms.frequency: Less common; more typical of advanced disease

symptoms.name: Fatigue
symptoms.detail: Persistent unexplained fatigue may be associated with advanced disease or bone metastases.
symptoms.frequency: Common in advanced disease; also an adverse effect of ADT

symptoms.name: Hematuria
symptoms.detail: Blood in the urine may occur with locally advanced disease involving the bladder or urethra.
symptoms.frequency: Uncommon; more typical of locally advanced disease

symptoms.name: Pathologic fracture
symptoms.detail: Fractures occurring with minimal trauma in the setting of bone metastases. May be the initial presentation of metastatic disease.
symptoms.frequency: Uncommon; seen in advanced bone metastatic disease

signs.name: Abnormal digital rectal examination (DRE)
signs.detail: DRE findings may include a firm or hard nodule in the prostate, asymmetry, or fixed induration suggesting extracapsular extension. The prostate may feel normal in many cases of early-stage disease.
signs.frequency: Variable; many early cancers are not palpable on DRE (cT1c)

signs.name: Elevated prostate-specific antigen (PSA)
signs.detail: PSA levels above the age-specific reference ranges. Normal PSA is generally considered <4 ng/mL, though elevated PSA can result from benign conditions (BPH, prostatitis) as well as malignancy.
signs.frequency: Most common finding leading to diagnosis; elevated PSA is the primary screening tool

signs.name: Elevated PSA density
signs.detail: PSA density (PSA divided by prostate volume) ≥0.15 ng/mL/g is associated with increased probability of clinically significant cancer and higher risk of grade reclassification during active surveillance.
signs.frequency: Used as an additional risk stratification tool

signs.name: Abnormal multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) findings
signs.detail: mpMRI with PI-RADS scoring can identify suspicious lesions. PI-RADS 4 or 5 lesions have increased risk of biopsy progression during active surveillance.

atypical_or_missed: Prostate cancer may be entirely asymptomatic and discovered only through elevated PSA or abnormal DRE during routine screening. Many patients with early-stage disease have no signs or symptoms.

Occult metastatic disease at initial presentation: Bone scans are rarely positive in asymptomatic patients with PSA <10 ng/mL, which may lead to understaging if imaging is not appropriately performed for intermediate-risk and higher disease.
Small cell neuroendocrine prostate cancer (NEPC) may present with low PSA despite large metastatic burden and visceral disease, potentially delaying diagnosis.
Intraductal carcinoma of the prostate (IDC-P) and cribriform histology may be associated with aggressive disease but may not be recognized without careful pathological evaluation.
De novo small cell carcinoma in untreated prostate cancer occurs rarely and is very aggressive.

Rare patients may have exposure to docetaxel without prior ARPI (ie, in the setting of EBRT to the primary tumor for low-volume synchronous mCSPC), which may complicate subsequent treatment decisions.

red_flags: Symptoms consistent with bone metastases (bone pain, unexplained weight loss, pathologic fracture) should prompt bone imaging regardless of PSA level
Rapidly rising PSA or short PSA doubling time (PSADT ≤8-10 months) suggesting aggressive disease biology
PSA persistence after radical prostatectomy (PSA does not fall to undetectable levels) indicating residual disease
PSA increase by ≥2 ng/mL above nadir after radiation therapy (Phoenix definition) indicating biochemical recurrence
New neurological symptoms suggesting spinal cord compression from vertebral metastases
Visceral metastases (liver, lung, adrenal gland, peritoneum, brain) indicating more aggressive disease requiring urgent systemic therapy evaluation
Symptomatic skeletal-related events: fractures, spinal cord compression, or need for surgery or radiation to bone

Clinical features of aggressive variant prostate cancer: visceral metastases, low PSA with bulky disease, high lactate dehydrogenase (LDH), high carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA), lytic bone metastases, or neuroendocrine histology

Clinical features suggesting treatment-related small cell/neuroendocrine prostate cancer: disease that no longer responds to ADT with positive metastases, associated with low PSA despite large metastatic burden and visceral disease, especially in patients with initial Grade Group 5
Evidence of castration-resistant progression: clinical, radiographic, or biochemical progression despite castrate levels of serum testosterone (<50 ng/dL)

diagnostic.rationale: Primary screening and monitoring tool for prostate cancer. Elevated PSA is the most common finding leading to diagnosis.
diagnostic.key_findings: PSA >4 ng/mL warrants further evaluation; PSA density ≥0.15 ng/mL/g increases risk of clinically significant cancer; PSA doubling time (PSADT) is prognostic for progression

diagnostic.rationale: Physical examination to assess prostate size, consistency, and clinical stage. Performed to confirm clinical stage.
diagnostic.key_findings: Firm nodules, asymmetry, or induration may suggest cancer; T-staging assessment (cT1 vs cT2 vs cT3)

diagnostic.rationale: Definitive diagnostic test required for all patients. Pathologist assigns Gleason primary and secondary grade and Grade Group.
diagnostic.key_findings: Gleason score and Grade Group (1-5), number of positive cores, percentage of core involvement, histologic variants (cribriform, intraductal, ductal), number of positive cores

diagnostic.rationale: PI-RADS scoring for risk stratification; helps detect clinically significant cancers (Grade Group ≥2), aids in staging (T-staging for extracapsular extension), and guides targeted biopsy.
diagnostic.key_findings: PI-RADS 4 or 5 lesions have increased risk of biopsy progression; mpMRI has high negative predictive value for extracapsular extension in low-risk disease

diagnostic.rationale: Comprehensive pathology reporting per College of American Pathologists (CAP) protocols.
diagnostic.key_findings: Complete Gleason grading, Grade Group, tumor extent, surgical margins, histologic variants, perineural invasion, lymphovascular invasion

staging.test: Bone imaging (bone scan or PSMA-PET/CT or PSMA-PET/MRI)
staging.rationale: Assess for osseous metastases. Indicated for patients with unfavorable intermediate, high, very-high risk disease (PROS-2), regional disease (PROS-7), and any patient with symptoms consistent with bone metastases.
staging.indication: Symptoms consistent with bone metastases, unfavorable intermediate-risk, high-risk, very-high-risk, regional, and metastatic disease; PSADT ≤8 months warrants more frequent imaging

staging.test: CT scan (chest, abdomen, pelvis with contrast)
staging.rationale: Soft tissue imaging to assess for lymph node metastases, visceral metastases, and extracapsular extension. May be performed with IV contrast.
staging.indication: Intermediate, high, and very-high risk disease; regional and metastatic disease; workup for recurrence or progression

staging.rationale: Preferred over CT for pelvic staging in many settings; high soft tissue contrast for assessment of local extension and lymphadenopathy.
staging.indication: Staging of intermediate to very-high risk disease; assessment of local recurrence after radiation

staging.rationale: Whole-body imaging with PSMA tracers (F-18 piflufolastat PSMA, F-18 flotufolastat PSMA, or Ga-68 PSMA-11) can be considered as an alternative to CT, MRI, and bone scans for initial staging of unfavorable intermediate, high, and very-high-risk disease. Higher sensitivity than C-11 choline or F-18 fluciclovine PET, especially at very low PSA levels.
staging.indication: Unfavorable intermediate, high, and very-high risk disease (initial staging); detection of biochemically recurrent disease; in M1 CRPC setting, only to determine candidacy for Lu-177-PSMA-617

staging.rationale: Not routinely recommended for staging prostate cancer due to limited data. May be prognostic in progressive CRPC. Useful in combination with PSMA-PET for biological heterogeneity assessment.
staging.indication: Not routinely recommended; may be considered in research or advanced CRPC settings

staging.test: F-18 fluciclovine PET/CT or C-11 choline PET/CT
staging.rationale: Can be used to detect small-volume recurrent disease in soft tissues and bone. Considered for equivocal results on initial bone scan.
staging.indication: Detection of biochemically recurrent disease; equivocal results on initial bone scan

staging.rationale: Targets osteoblast activity; can be considered for equivocal results on initial bone scan. Higher sensitivity than standard bone scan.
staging.indication: Equivocal results on initial bone scan

staging.rationale: Can be performed with radical prostatectomy for staging and prognostic information. Extended PLND provides more complete staging. May cure some patients with microscopic metastases.
staging.indication: Recommended with RP for unfavorable intermediate, high, very-high risk, and regional disease; considered for favorable intermediate risk

biomarkers.rationale: Testing for hereditary cancer syndromes including BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, CHEK2, and mismatch repair genes. Implications for family risk assessment, other cancer screening, cascade testing for relatives, and potential treatment implications.
biomarkers.when: At time of initial diagnosis for patients with regional, very-high-risk, high-risk, or metastatic disease; also at recurrence if not previously done

biomarkers.test: Somatic tumor testing for homologous recombination repair (HRR) gene mutations
biomarkers.rationale: Testing for mutations in BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, PALB2, FANCA, RAD51D, CHEK2, CDK12 and other HRR genes. BRCA1/2 and BRCA2 mutations in particular are associated with response to PARP inhibitor therapy.
biomarkers.when: Recommended for metastatic disease; can be considered for regional (N1) disease

biomarkers.test: Tumor testing for MSI-H or dMMR
biomarkers.rationale: Microsatellite instability-high or mismatch repair deficient testing. If positive, indicates eligibility for pembrolizumab in mCRPC and warrants referral for genetic counseling to assess for Lynch syndrome.
biomarkers.when: Recommended in mCRPC; can be considered in regional or castration-sensitive metastatic disease

biomarkers.rationale: TMB ≥10 mut/mB may indicate potential benefit from pembrolizumab in mCRPC based on limited data.

Alternative to metastatic biopsy when biopsy is unsafe or unfeasible. Should preferably be collected during biochemical and/or radiographic progression to maximize diagnostic yield. Caution needed due to potential interference from clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP).
biomarkers.when: When metastatic biopsy is unsafe or unfeasible; collected during progression

biomarkers.rationale: Calculated as PSA divided by prostate volume. PSADT (PSA doubling time) is important for risk stratification and treatment decisions, particularly in the BCR and CRPC settings.
biomarkers.when: At initial diagnosis and during follow-up; critical in BCR and CRPC settings

do_not_miss: Metastatic lesion biopsy for histologic and molecular evaluation in mCRPC (to rule out small cell/neuroendocrine histology)
Bone imaging for patients with symptoms consistent with bone metastases regardless of PSA level
Soft tissue imaging (CT or MRI) for intermediate, high, very-high risk, regional, and metastatic disease
Germline testing for patients with regional, very-high-risk, high-risk, or metastatic disease
Somatic testing for HRR mutations, MSI-H/dMMR, and TMB in metastatic disease
Document castrate levels of testosterone (<50 ng/dL) when evaluating for progression on ADT
avoid: Routine FDG-PET/CT for initial staging of prostate cancer (limited data in this setting)
Imaging for patients with low- and intermediate-risk disease and life expectancy ≤5 years until symptoms develop
Biopsy of bone lesions that are clearly benign on imaging
Use of PSMA-PET in M0 CRPC to change systemic therapy (changes should not be made solely based on positive PSMA-PET in M0 CRPC)
Biliary duct or renal pelvis imaging in the absence of specific symptoms suggesting involvement

staging.system: AJCC 8th Edition (2017) TNM Staging System for Prostate Cancer. Clinical stage is based on TNM classification. Prognostic stage groups incorporate TNM stage, PSA level, and Grade Group.

t_stages.description: Clinical assessment of primary tumor based on DRE and imaging
Primary tumor cannot be assessed
No evidence of primary tumor
Clinically inapparent tumor that is not palpable
Tumor incidental histologic finding in 5% or less of tissue resected
Tumor incidental histologic finding in more than 5% of tissue resected
Tumor identified by needle biopsy found in one or both sides, but not palpable
Tumor is palpable and confined within prostate
Tumor involves one-half of one side or less
Tumor involves more than one-half of one side but not both sides
Tumor involves both sides
Extraprostatic tumor that is not fixed or does not invade adjacent structures
Extraprostatic extension (unilateral or bilateral)
Tumor invades seminal vesicle(s)
Tumor is fixed or invades adjacent structures other than seminal vesicles such as external sphincter, rectum, bladder, levator muscles, and/or pelvic wall
Pathological assessment of primary tumor after surgery. Note: There is no pathological T1 classification.
Organ confined
Extraprostatic extension
Extraprostatic extension (unilateral or bilateral) or microscopic invasion of bladder neck
Tumor invades seminal vesicle(s)
Tumor is fixed or invades adjacent structures other than seminal vesicles such as external sphincter, rectum, bladder, levator muscles, and/or pelvic wall

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed

m_stages.description: Other site(s) with or without bone disease. Note: When more than one site of metastasis is present, the most advanced category is used. M1c is most advanced.

stage_groups.criteria: cT1a-c, N0, M0, PSA <10, Grade Group 1
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Very early, organ-confined, low-grade cancer. Active surveillance preferred.

stage_groups.criteria: cT1a-c, N0, M0, PSA ≥10 to <20, Grade Group 1; OR cT2a, N0, M0, PSA ≥10 to <20, Grade Group 1; OR pT2, N0, M0, PSA ≥10 to <20, Grade Group 1; OR cT2b, N0, M0, PSA <20, Grade Group 1; OR cT2c, N0, M0, PSA <20, Grade Group 1
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Low to intermediate risk; organ confined with low grade or higher stage with very low PSA.

stage_groups.criteria: T1-2, N0, M0, PSA <20, Grade Group 2
T1-2, N0, M0, PSA <20, Grade Group 3; OR T1-2, N0, M0, PSA <20, Grade Group 4
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Higher intermediate to high risk with aggressive histology.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Good to excellent depending on specific features

stage_groups.criteria: T1-2, N0, M0, PSA ≥20, Grade Group 1-4
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Intermediate to high risk based on elevated PSA.

stage_groups.criteria: T3-4, N0, M0, Any PSA, Grade Group 1-4
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Locally advanced disease with extraprostatic extension or invasion of adjacent structures.

stage_groups.criteria: Any T, N0, M0, Any PSA, Grade Group 5
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Very high risk with Gleason 9-10 histology.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Variable; depends on completeness of response to multimodality therapy

stage_groups.criteria: Any T, N1, M0, Any PSA, Any Grade Group
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Regional disease with pelvic lymph node metastases.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Variable; depends on response to systemic therapy
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative or palliative depending on extent

stage_groups.criteria: Any T, Any N, M1, Any PSA, Any Grade Group
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Variable; improved with modern systemic therapies
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative; systemic therapy with intent to prolong survival

staging_pearls: The NCCN guidelines use NCCN risk group classification (low, favorable intermediate, unfavorable intermediate, high, very high) rather than AJCC prognostic staging groups for treatment recommendations.

Grade Group system (ISUP 2014): Grade Group 1 = Gleason ≤6; Grade Group 2 = Gleason 3+4=7; Grade Group 3 = Gleason 4+3=7; Grade Group 4 = Gleason 4+4=8 or 3+5=8 or 5+3=8; Grade Group 5 = Gleason 9-10.
High-volume mCSPC is defined based on CHAARTED criteria: presence of visceral metastases or ≥4 bone lesions with ≥1 beyond the vertebral bodies and pelvis.
Low-volume mCSPC: defined as not meeting high-volume criteria; may include patients with ≤5 bony metastases without visceral metastases.
Metachronous oligometastatic disease: metastatic disease detected after prior definitive therapy, distinguishable from synchronous metastatic disease.

PSA persistence after RP is defined as when PSA does not fall to undetectable levels; PSA recurrence after RP is undetectable PSA after RP with subsequent detectable PSA that increases on ≥2 determinations or increases to PSA >0.1 ng/mL.
PSA recurrence after radiation (Phoenix definition): PSA increase by ≥2 ng/mL above the nadir PSA.
CRPC is defined as prostate cancer that progresses clinically, radiographically, or biochemically despite castrate levels of serum testosterone (<50 ng/dL).
Adverse pathologic features include: positive margin(s), seminal vesicle invasion, or extracapsular extension.

Small cell neuroendocrine prostate cancer (NEPC) may be present in up to 17% of mCRPC patients; histologic evidence of both adenocarcinoma and small cell carcinoma may be present in the same specimen.

PSMA-PET imaging should only be used in the setting of M1 CRPC to determine if a patient is a candidate for Lu-177-PSMA-617; changes in systemic therapy should not be made solely based on a positive PSMA-PET in patients with M0 CRPC.
The Will Rogers phenomenon: improved imaging (such as PSMA-PET) may upstage patients from M0 to M1b, potentially improving outcomes in both groups without any actual change in treatment efficacy.
When more than one site of metastasis is present, the most advanced M category is used (M1c is most advanced).
This staging classification applies to adenocarcinomas and squamous carcinomas, but not to sarcoma or transitional cell (urothelial) carcinoma of the prostate.
Adjectives used to describe histologic variants include mucinous, signet ring cell, ductal, and neuroendocrine, including small cell carcinoma. Histologic confirmation of disease is required.
Positive surgical margin should be indicated by an R1 descriptor, indicating residual microscopic disease.

Life expectancy estimation is critical for treatment decisions. The Social Security Administration tables, MSK Male Life Expectancy tool, or UCSF Lee Schonberg Index can be used, adjusted by adding or subtracting 50% based on the patient's overall health quartile.

management_principles.overview: Prostate cancer treatment is guided by risk stratification using TNM staging, Gleason grade group, and PSA level. The NCCN Panel employs a multimodal approach where treatment decisions are personalized based on disease characteristics, life expectancy, comorbidities, and patient preferences [PROS-1, PROS-2]. Treatment philosophy emphasizes avoiding overtreatment of indolent disease through active surveillance in low-risk patients while ensuring adequate treatment intensification in high-risk and metastatic disease states. All recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise indicated.

by_intent.patient_group: Clinically localized disease (N0, M0) with life expectancy ≥10 years
by_intent.approach: Active surveillance (preferred for low-risk), radical prostatectomy, or radiation therapy with or without ADT depending on risk group

by_intent.patient_group: Regional disease (N1, M0) or high/very-high-risk localized disease
by_intent.approach: RT plus long-term ADT (18-36 months), with abiraterone for very-high-risk or N1 disease; or RP plus PLND in select patients
ADT with treatment intensification using ARPIs and/or docetaxel; MDT for oligometastatic disease
Sequential systemic therapies based on prior ARPI and docetaxel exposure, including PARP inhibitors for HRRm, Lu-177-PSMA-617, radium-223, and chemotherapy

management_principles.mdt_requirements: All patients diagnosed with intermediate-, high-, very-high-, regional-risk, low-volume mCSPC, biochemically recurrent, and oligometastatic mCRPC are recommended to be evaluated by a radiation oncologist as part of a multidisciplinary discussion [PROS-J]. Multidisciplinary tumor board review is essential for complex clinical scenarios including node-positive disease, biochemical recurrence, and metastatic disease where multiple treatment modalities may be considered [PROS-D].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Life expectancy estimation is critical to informed decision-making and is determined using Social Security Administration tables, WHO Life Tables, MSK Male Life Expectancy tool, or UCSF Lee Schonberg Index, adjusted by +50% for best quartile health and -50% for worst quartile health [PROS-A]. For patients with life expectancy ≤5 years with low- and intermediate-risk disease, no imaging or treatment is indicated until symptoms develop. For those with high/very-high risk and life expectancy ≤5 years, observation may be appropriate if asymptomatic, though bone imaging should be performed if symptomatic [PROS-2A footnote j]. ECOG performance status 0-1 is required for sipuleucel-T [PROS-N footnote ppp].

nodes.criteria: NCCN Low-risk: cT1-cT2a, Grade Group 1, PSA <10 ng/mL; life expectancy ≥10 years

options.label: Active surveillance (preferred)
options.recommendation: Active surveillance is preferred for most patients. Confirmatory testing within 6-12 months recommended. Monitor with PSA every 6 months, DRE every 12 months, repeat biopsy every 1-3 years, consider mpMRI every 12 months [PROS-F]

options.label: Radiation therapy (RT)
options.recommendation: EBRT or brachytherapy as monotherapy without ENI, without ADT, without brachytherapy boost [PROS-J table 1]

options.label: Radical prostatectomy (RP)
options.recommendation: RP is appropriate for patients with life expectancy ≥10 years. Consider PLND if favorable intermediate-risk features [PROS-K]

adjuvant_logic.summary: No ADT recommended for low-risk disease with RT

nodes.criteria: Low-risk, life expectancy ≤5 years, asymptomatic

options.label: Observation
options.recommendation: No imaging or treatment until symptomatic, then ADT should be given [PROS-2A footnote j]

adjuvant_logic.summary: ADT monotherapy only for symptomatic progression

nodes.criteria: Favorable intermediate-risk: 1 IRF, Grade Group 1-2, <50% positive cores; life expectancy >10 years

options.label: Active surveillance
options.recommendation: Consider active surveillance particularly for those with low percentage pattern 4, low tumor volume, low PSA density, and/or low genomic risk. Not recommended for patients with unfavorable histology (expansile/large cribriform, intraductal carcinoma) [PROS-F]. Patients should be counseled that unselected Grade Group 2 IR disease has unacceptably high risk of metastasis on active surveillance

options.label: Radiation therapy (RT) without ADT
options.recommendation: EBRT or brachytherapy monotherapy without ENI, without brachytherapy boost. ADT is generally not recommended but can be considered if additional risk assessments suggest aggressive behavior [PROS-J]

options.label: Radical prostatectomy (RP)
options.recommendation: RP appropriate with life expectancy ≥10 years. Consider PLND [PROS-K]

adjuvant_logic.summary: ADT is not recommended with RT for most FIR patients; if given, short-term (4-6 months) only [PROS-G]

nodes.criteria: FIR, life expectancy ≤5 years, asymptomatic

options.label: Observation (preferred)
options.recommendation: Observation is preferred for asymptomatic patients with life expectancy ≤5 years [PROS-F]

adjuvant_logic.summary: No adjuvant therapy in observation setting

nodes.criteria: Unfavorable intermediate-risk: ≥2-3 IRFs, or Grade Group 3, or ≥50% positive cores; life expectancy >10 years

options.label: RT + short-term ADT (4-6 months)
options.recommendation: EBRT, brachytherapy monotherapy, or EBRT with brachytherapy boost. ST-ADT (4-6 months) recommended unless additional risk assessments suggest less aggressive tumor or if medically contraindicated. Concurrent/adjuvant ADT preferred over neoadjuvant ADT [PROS-J, PROS-G]. ENI should not be used routinely. Focal boosting with isotoxic delivery can be considered [PROS-J]

options.label: Radical prostatectomy (RP)
options.recommendation: RP appropriate with life expectancy ≥10 years. Consider extended PLND [PROS-K]

adjuvant_logic.summary: ST-ADT (4-6 months) is recommended as concurrent/adjuvant with RT unless medically contraindicated or risk assessments suggest low benefit

nodes.criteria: UIR, no evidence of metastases on staging, life expectancy >5 years but considered for observation

options.label: Monitoring
options.recommendation: Active surveillance of unfavorable intermediate and high-risk disease is NOT recommended in patients with life expectancy >10 years (category 1) [PROS-7A footnote z]. Monitoring is not preferred for patients with multiple high-risk features [PROS-7A footnote cc]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Active surveillance not recommended for UIR with life expectancy >10 years

nodes.criteria: High-risk: cT3-T4, Grade Group 4-5, or PSA >20 ng/mL; Life expectancy >5 years or symptomatic

options.label: RT + long-term ADT (12-36 months)
options.recommendation: EBRT with LT-ADT (12-36 months) is category 1. For high-risk treated with combination EBRT + brachytherapy, shortened ADT (12 months) can be considered [PROS-J, PROS-G]. ENI is at physician discretion. Focal boosting can be considered

options.label: RP + PLND
options.recommendation: RP + PLND can be considered in younger, healthier patients without tumor fixation to pelvic sidewall. RP for high-risk should be viewed as one component of a multimodality approach [PROS-7A footnote bb, PROS-K]

adjuvant_logic.summary: LT-ADT (12-36 months) is category 1 for high-risk disease. RT dose or modality has not demonstrated ability to obviate benefit of LT-ADT [PROS-J]

nodes.criteria: Very-high-risk: at least 2 of cT3-T4, Grade Group 4-5, PSA >40 ng/mL; Life expectancy >5 years or symptomatic

options.label: RT + LT-ADT (18-36 months)
options.recommendation: EBRT with LT-ADT (18-36 months). Addition of abiraterone is very selective as benefit in contemporary practice is uncertain [PROS-J]. Combination brachytherapy boost with EBRT should NOT be used routinely (excluded from ASCENDE-RT if PSA >40 or T3b) [PROS-J]

adjuvant_logic.summary: LT-ADT (18-36 months) for very-high-risk. Abiraterone addition is very selective and NOT recommended routinely [PROS-J]

nodes.criteria: High/very-high risk, life expectancy ≤5 years, asymptomatic

options.label: Observation ± ADT
options.recommendation: Observation is reasonable for asymptomatic patients with life expectancy ≤5 years. If symptomatic progression, initiate ADT [PROS-6]

adjuvant_logic.summary: ADT monotherapy for symptomatic progression only

nodes.criteria: N1M0, life expectancy >5 years or symptomatic

options.label: RT + ADT (24-36 months) + abiraterone (preferred) or ADT ± abiraterone
options.recommendation: RT + ADT (24 months) + abiraterone is preferred based on STAMPEDE data. Alternatively RT + ADT (24-36 months) ± abiraterone [PROS-7, PROS-J]. RP + PLND may be considered in select patients with >10-year life expectancy and resectable disease [PROS-7A footnote dd]

adjuvant_logic.summary: LT-ADT (24-36 months) plus abiraterone is preferred for N1M0 based on STAMPEDE. RT to primary tumor is recommended [PROS-J]

nodes.criteria: N1M0, life expectancy ≤5 years, asymptomatic

options.label: Observation or ADT ± RT
options.recommendation: For asymptomatic patients with life expectancy ≤5 years, observation may be considered. If symptomatic or progression on observation, ADT ± RT [PROS-6]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Observation until symptomatic progression

nodes.criteria: Life expectancy >5 years, no evidence of M1 on staging

options.label: Secondary EBRT (preferred) ± ADT (± abiraterone for N1, category 2B)
options.recommendation: Early secondary RT at PSA 0.1-0.2 ng/mL is recommended. If ADT is given, duration 6-24 months. ADT duration should be personalized based on pre-RT PSA, clinicopathologic risk, age, life expectancy, comorbidities, preferences [PROS-9, PROS-J]. Consider prostate bed biopsy

options.label: Monitoring
options.recommendation: Monitoring with physical exam, PSA every 3-6 months, imaging for symptoms or increasing PSA. Patients with prolonged PSADTs (>12 months) and older age may be candidates [PROS-9A footnote kk,ll]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Short-term ADT generally recommended for sRT; LT-ADT may be preferred for late sRT, multiple adverse features, or lymph node involvement. No clear OS benefit from ADT added to early sRT [PROS-J]

nodes.criteria: Life expectancy >5 years, M1 disease

options.label: Treat as metachronous metastatic disease
options.recommendation: Workup and treatment per PROS-13 (metachronous oligometastatic) or PROS-14/15 (metachronous low/high-volume M1 CSPC)

adjuvant_logic.summary: Metastatic disease pathway applies

nodes.criteria: Life expectancy ≤5 years

options.label: Observation or palliative therapy
options.recommendation: Observation preferred. If progression, best supportive care or palliative therapy [PROS-9A]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Palliative approach for limited life expectancy

nodes.criteria: PSA recurrence or positive DRE after RT, life expectancy >5 years, no M1

options.label: Local secondary therapy ± ADT
options.recommendation: ADT based on PSADT. For local secondary therapy: cryotherapy, HIFU, IRE (category 2B), reirradiation (LDR/HDR brachytherapy, SBRT), or RP + PLND. Risk stratification and bone/soft tissue imaging required [PROS-L]. Monitor also an option

options.label: Monitoring
options.recommendation: Monitoring with physical exam, PSA every 3-6 months, imaging for symptoms or increasing PSA [PROS-10A footnote kk,ll]

adjuvant_logic.summary: ADT should not be reflexively ordered [PROS-L]. Treatment should be individualized based on risk of progression

nodes.criteria: PSA recurrence, M1 disease

options.label: Treat as metastatic disease
options.recommendation: Workup and treatment per metastatic pathways (PROS-13, 14, or 15)

adjuvant_logic.summary: Metastatic disease pathway applies

nodes.criteria: Life expectancy ≤5 years

options.label: Observation or palliative therapy
options.recommendation: Observation preferred. If progression, best supportive care [PROS-10A]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Palliative approach

nodes.criteria: BCR2, N0M0 CSPC, low-risk BCR (PSADT >9 months, or no high-risk features)

options.label: Monitoring (preferred)
options.recommendation: Monitoring until diagnosis of metastatic disease is preferred for low-risk BCR2 [PROS-12 footnote pp]. PSA level and PSADT should be considered when deciding whether to begin ADT

options.label: ADT
options.recommendation: ADT monotherapy is an option. Intermittent ADT can be considered if favorable PSA response [PROS-12 footnote qq]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Monitoring preferred for low-risk BCR2

nodes.criteria: BCR2, N0M0 CSPC, high-risk BCR (PSADT ≤9 months)

options.label: Enzalutamide ± leuprolide
options.recommendation: For high-risk BCR2: Enzalutamide ± leuprolide. Eligibility: M0 by CT/MRI/bone scan; PSADT ≤9 months; PSA ≥2 ng/mL above nadir after RT or ≥1 ng/mL after RP; not candidate for pelvic-directed therapy [PROS-G footnote d, EMBARK trial]

options.label: Apalutamide + ADT (category 2B)
options.recommendation: For high-risk BCR2 after RP: PSADT ≤9 months; PSA ≥0.5 ng/mL; prior adjuvant or secondary RT or not candidate for RT [PROS-G footnote e, PRESTO trial]

options.label: ADT
options.recommendation: ADT monotherapy is an option for high-risk BCR2 [PROS-12]

options.label: Monitoring
options.recommendation: Monitoring and ADT are equally reasonable options for high-risk BCR2, and shared decision-making is key [PROS-12]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treatment intensification options available for high-risk BCR2 based on EMBARK and PRESTO trials

nodes.criteria: Metachronous oligorecurrent disease, ≤5 metastases by conventional imaging or ≤10 by PSMA-PET

options.label: MDT (preferred) ± ADT
options.recommendation: MDT with SBRT is preferred. ADT can be added (6 months). Use MDT to delay ADT initiation or improve PFS. Goal is to treat all metastatic sites including primary if untreated or local recurrence [PROS-M, PROS-13]. Trials: ORIOLE, STOMP, EXTEND, RADIOSA

options.label: ADT + systemic therapy for low-volume mCSPC
options.recommendation: ADT with ARPI (abiraterone, apalutamide, enzalutamide preferred; darolutamide other recommended). Consider concurrent MDT [PROS-14]

adjuvant_logic.summary: MDT with ADT improved PFS (RADIOSA trial: HR 0.43). MDT alone or with intermittent ADT is preferred. Continuous ADT may also be considered [PROS-13]

nodes.criteria: M1 progression on ADT ± ARPI

options.label: Workup and treatment of M1 CRPC
options.recommendation: Proceed to PROS-17 for M1 CRPC management

adjuvant_logic.summary: Progression to CRPC pathway

management_pathways.title: Low-Volume M1 CSPC - Synchronous or Metachronous (PROS-14)

nodes.criteria: Synchronous low-volume metastases or synchronous oligometastatic disease

options.label: ADT + ARPI (preferred: abiraterone/cat1, apalutamide/cat1, enzalutamide/cat1; other: darolutamide/cat2B)
options.recommendation: ADT with one ARPI. Category 1 for abiraterone, apalutamide, enzalutamide [PROS-14]

options.label: ADT + docetaxel + ARPI (low-volume only, category 2B)
options.recommendation: For fit patients desiring aggressive treatment. Controversial in low-volume disease; 78% of AMPLITUDE trial patients had high-volume disease [PROS-14]

options.label: ADT + EBRT to primary tumor ± ARPI or docetaxel
options.recommendation: EBRT to primary tumor can improve OS in low metastatic burden (CHAARTED low-volume criteria: <4 bone metastases without visceral disease) [PROS-14, STAMPEDE]. Can be combined with abiraterone (PEACE-1) [PROS-J]

options.label: ADT + niraparib/abiraterone (BRCA2m only, category 2B)
options.recommendation: Only for BRCA2-mutated mCSPC. rPFS improved in BRCAm population; OS data immature. Controversial in low-volume disease due to toxicity. Can be used following docetaxel or EBRT to primary tumor [PROS-14 footnote bbb, PROS-G]

adjuvant_logic.summary: EBRT to primary tumor associated with OS benefit in low metastatic burden [STAMPEDE]. Concurrent MDT can be considered in select oligometastatic patients [PROS-15A footnote zz]

nodes.criteria: Metachronous low-volume metastases

options.label: ADT + ARPI (preferred: abiraterone/cat1, apalutamide/cat1, enzalutamide/cat1; other: darolutamide/cat2B)
options.recommendation: ADT with one ARPI [PROS-14]

options.label: ADT + niraparib/abiraterone (BRCA2m only, category 2B)
options.recommendation: Only for BRCA2-mutated mCSPC [PROS-14]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treatment for metachronous disease per same systemic options as synchronous low-volume

nodes.criteria: Progression on ADT ± ARPI

options.label: Workup and treatment of M1 CRPC
options.recommendation: Proceed to PROS-17

adjuvant_logic.summary: CRPC pathway applies

nodes.criteria: Synchronous or metachronous high-volume metastases (CHAARTED criteria: visceral metastases OR ≥4 bone metastases with ≥1 beyond vertebral bodies and pelvis)

options.label: ADT + docetaxel + ARPI (preferred: abiraterone/cat1, darolutamide/cat1; other: apalutamide/cat2B, enzalutamide/cat2B)
options.recommendation: Triplet therapy recommended for fit patients with high-volume disease. Based on PEACE-1 and ARASENS trials showing OS improvement over ADT+docetaxel [PROS-15]

options.label: ADT + ARPI (preferred: abiraterone/cat1, apalutamide/cat1, enzalutamide/cat1; other: darolutamide)
options.recommendation: Doublet therapy is also suitable for high-volume disease. No studies compared doublet vs triplet [PROS-15]

options.label: ADT + niraparib/abiraterone (BRCA2m only)
options.recommendation: For BRCA2-mutated mCSPC only. Can be used following docetaxel in triplet therapy or following EBRT to primary tumor [PROS-15 footnote bbb]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Triplet therapy (ADT+docetaxel+ARPI) is preferred for fit high-volume patients. Doublet therapy also appropriate [PROS-15]

nodes.criteria: High-volume mCSPC, select patients - EBRT to primary can be considered

options.label: ADT + EBRT to primary (category 2B)
options.recommendation: EBRT to primary tumor can be considered for select high-volume patients. PEACE-1 showed improved rPFS with RT to primary in those receiving abiraterone. Reduced severe genitourinary adverse events [PROS-J]

adjuvant_logic.summary: RT to primary in high-volume is category 2B and considered for select patients

nodes.criteria: Progression on ADT ± ARPI

options.label: Workup and treatment of M1 CRPC
options.recommendation: Proceed to PROS-17

adjuvant_logic.summary: CRPC pathway applies

nodes.criteria: M0 CRPC, PSADT >10 months

options.label: Monitoring (preferred)
options.recommendation: Monitoring with continued ADT is preferred. These patients will have relatively indolent disease history [PROS-16]

options.label: Continue hormone therapy
options.recommendation: Maintain castrate levels of testosterone (<50 ng/dL) [PROS-16]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Indolent disease, monitoring preferred for PSADT >10 months

nodes.criteria: M0 CRPC, PSADT ≤10 months, no metastases on imaging

options.label: Secondary hormone therapy with ADT
options.recommendation: Preferred options: Apalutamide (cat1), Darolutamide (cat1), Enzalutamide (cat1). Other recommended: other secondary hormone therapy (abiraterone, enzalutamide following prior ARPI) [PROS-16]

options.label: Maintain current treatment
options.recommendation: Maintain current treatment and consider periodic PSA assessment. If PSADT ≤10 mo, other secondary evidence of metastases may develop [PROS-16]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Treatment intensification for shorter PSADT

nodes.criteria: M0 CRPC, progression with metastases on imaging

options.label: Workup and treatment of M1 CRPC
options.recommendation: Proceed to PROS-17. Document castrate levels of testosterone (<50 ng/dL) [PROS-16 footnote ff]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Metastatic CRPC pathway applies

nodes.criteria: Pre-ARPI mCRPC

options.label: Abiraterone (category 1) or Enzalutamide (category 1) - Preferred
options.recommendation: First-line preferred for patients without prior ARPI [PROS-18]

options.label: Docetaxel (category 1) - Other Recommended
options.recommendation: For patients not receiving ARPI as first line [PROS-18]

options.label: PARP inhibitor/ARPI combination for BRCAm - Useful in Certain Circumstances
options.recommendation: BRCA mutation: niraparib/abiraterone (cat1), olaparib/abiraterone (cat1), talazoparib/enzalutamide (cat1). HRRm other than BRCA1/2: talazoparib/enzalutamide (cat1) [PROS-18]

options.label: Radium-223/enzalutamide for bone metastases - Useful in Certain Circumstances
options.recommendation: For bone metastases [PROS-18]

adjuvant_logic.summary: First-line options based on disease characteristics and biomarkers

nodes.criteria: Post-ARPI / Pre-Docetaxel mCRPC

options.label: Docetaxel (category 1) - Preferred
options.recommendation: Preferred after ARPI progression without prior docetaxel [PROS-18]

options.label: PARP inhibitor for BRCAm - Useful in Certain Circumstances
options.recommendation: BRCA: olaparib (cat1, preferred), rucaparib (cat1, preferred), niraparib/abiraterone (cat2B), talazoparib/enzalutamide (cat2B). HRRm: olaparib, talazoparib/enzalutamide (cat2B) [PROS-18]

options.label: Lu-177-PSMA-617 for PSMA-positive metastases
options.recommendation: For patients with PSMA-positive disease progressing on ARPI, considered appropriate to delay taxane chemotherapy [PROS-18, PSMAfore trial]

options.label: Cabazitaxel/carboplatin for aggressive variant
options.recommendation: For aggressive variant mCRPC (visceral metastases, high LDH, lytic bone, NEPC histology) or unfavorable genomics (defects in ≥2 of PTEN, TP53, RB1) [PROS-18, PROS-N]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Second-line options based on ARPI exposure, biomarkers, and disease state

nodes.criteria: Post-ARPI / Post-Docetaxel mCRPC

options.label: Cabazitaxel (category 1) or Docetaxel rechallenge - Preferred
options.recommendation: Cabazitaxel preferred. Docetaxel rechallenge can be attempted if no definitive progression on prior docetaxel [PROS-18, CARD trial]

options.label: PARP inhibitor for BRCAm - Useful in Certain Circumstances
options.recommendation: BRCA: olaparib (cat1), rucaparib. HRRm: olaparib. Other FDA-approved tissue-agnostic agents [PROS-18]

options.label: Lu-177-PSMA-617 for PSMA-positive metastases (category 1)
options.recommendation: Category 1 for post-ARPI and post-taxane patients with PSMA-positive disease [PROS-18, VISION trial]

options.label: Cabazitaxel/carboplatin for aggressive variant
options.recommendation: For aggressive variant disease [PROS-18]

options.label: Mitoxantrone for symptomatic palliation
options.recommendation: For symptomatic patients unable to tolerate other therapies [PROS-18]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Post-ARPI and post-docetaxel treatment options

nodes.criteria: Additional options irrespective of prior ARPI or docetaxel

options.label: Sipuleucel-T for asymptomatic without visceral metastases
options.recommendation: For asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic, no liver metastases, life expectancy >6 months, ECOG 0-1 [PROS-18 footnote ppp]. Not recommended if visceral metastases present

options.label: Pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR (category 2B)
options.recommendation: Pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR mCRPC. Limited data suggest benefit in TMB ≥10 mut/mB [PROS-18, PROS-N]

options.label: MDT for oligometastatic/oligoprogressive disease
options.recommendation: MDT with mCRPC systemic therapy for oligometastatic or oligoprogressive CRPC [PROS-M, PROS-18]

options.label: Radium-223 for symptomatic bone-predominant metastases (category 1)
options.recommendation: For symptomatic bone metastases, no visceral metastases. Concomitant denosumab or zoledronic acid recommended [PROS-18, PROS-J]. Should not be used with visceral metastases

adjuvant_logic.summary: Options available regardless of prior therapy based on disease characteristics and biomarkers

nodes.criteria: Small cell/neuroendocrine histology confirmed on metastatic biopsy, regardless of treatment history

options.label: Cisplatin/etoposide or Carboplatin/etoposide
options.recommendation: Treat as per NCCN Guidelines for Small Cell Lung Cancer. Additional options include docetaxel/carboplatin and cabazitaxel/carboplatin [PROS-17]

options.label: Docetaxel/carboplatin or Cabazitaxel/carboplatin
options.recommendation: Alternative chemotherapy regimens [PROS-17]

options.label: Palliative RT for painful bone metastases
options.recommendation: Palliative radiation for symptomatic bone metastases [PROS-17]

options.label: Best supportive care
options.recommendation: Best supportive care is always an appropriate option [PROS-17]

adjuvant_logic.summary: Small cell/NEPC treated differently from adenocarcinoma; consult NCCN Small Cell Lung Cancer guidelines

items.text: Physical examination and digital rectal examination (DRE) to confirm clinical stage
Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) measurement
Review diagnostic prostate biopsies
Estimate life expectancy (Principles of Life Expectancy Estimation [PROS-A])
Inquire about known high-risk germline mutations and family history
Perform somatic and/or germline testing as appropriate
Assess quality-of-life measures
NCCN risk group assignment based on TNM stage, Grade Group, and PSA level
For low-risk: Confirmatory testing can be used to assess appropriateness of active surveillance
For favorable intermediate-risk: Confirmatory testing can be used for active surveillance assessment
For unfavorable intermediate/high/very-high risk: Soft tissue imaging (mpMRI preferred over CT for pelvic staging); consider bone imaging
PSMA-PET/CT or PET/MRI can be considered as alternative to CT, MRI, and bone scans for initial staging of unfavorable intermediate-, high-, and very-high-risk disease
If regional metastases found: see Regional Prostate Cancer (PROS-7)
If distant metastases found: see Low-Volume M1 (PROS-14) or High-Volume M1 (PROS-15)
Bone imaging should be performed for any patient with symptoms consistent with bone metastases
Bone and soft tissue imaging for staging
Consider DRE to confirm clinical stage
Calculate PSA doubling time (PSADT)
Estimate life expectancy
Germline and somatic genetic testing
Obtain family history
Assess quality-of-life measures
Bone scan (technetium-99m-MDP) for bone metastasis evaluation
items.recommendation: Recommended for high-risk patients and those with symptoms [PROS-E]

items.text: PSMA-PET/CT or PSMA-PET/MRI as alternative to CT/MRI/bone scan for initial staging (unfavorable intermediate, high, very-high risk)
Multiparametric MRI (mpMRI) for pelvic staging preferred over CT; equivalent to CT for lymph node evaluation
Chest CT for staging in unfavorable intermediate through very-high risk

Consider plain films, CT, MRI, or PET with F-18 piflufolastat PSMA, Ga-68 PSMA-11, F-18 flotufolastat PSMA, F-18 fluciclovine, F-18 sodium fluoride, or C-11 choline for equivocal results on initial bone scan
PSMA-PET imaging should only be used in M1 CRPC setting to determine candidacy for Lu-177-PSMA-617
Changes in systemic therapy should NOT be made solely based on positive PSMA-PET in M0 CRPC
Fluorodeoxyglucose (FDG)-PET/CT should NOT be used routinely for staging prostate cancer
Germline testing: Inquire about family/personal history and known germline variants at initial diagnosis and recurrence [CRIT-6, HRS-3]
items.recommendation: Recommended for metastatic, regional, very-high-risk, or high-risk localized disease [PROS-C]

items.text: Somatic tumor testing for HRR gene mutations (BRCA1, BRCA2, ATM, PALB2, FANCA, RAD51D, CHEK2, CDK12) in metastatic disease; consider in regional disease
Tumor testing for MSI-H or dMMR in mCRPC; consider in regional or mCSPC
TMB testing in mCRPC
Metastatic biopsy strongly recommended for histologic and molecular evaluation; plasma ctDNA assay if biopsy unsafe/unfeasible
Circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) collection preferably during biochemical/radiographic progression to maximize yield
Diagnostic prostate biopsies with Gleason primary and secondary grade assignment
Grade Group classification (ISUP 2014)
Pathology synoptic reports per College of American Pathologists (CAP) compliant with CoC requirements
Metastatic biopsy for histologic and molecular evaluation including small cell/neuroendocrine features
Quality-of-life measures assessment using standardized patient-reported outcomes instrument (e.g., EPIC-26)
Consider geriatric assessment (see NCCN Guidelines for Older Adult Oncology)
Refer to NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List (includes social determinants of health)
Radiation oncology evaluation for intermediate, high, very-high, regional risk, low-volume mCSPC, biochemically recurrent, and oligometastatic mCRPC
Baseline DEXA scan before starting ADT in patients at increased fracture risk based on FRAX
Comprehensive dental evaluation before initiating osteoclast inhibitor
Baseline urinary, sexual, and bowel function measurement

surgery.role: Radical prostatectomy is an appropriate curative treatment for clinically localized prostate cancer in patients with life expectancy ≥10 years without serious comorbidities contraindicating elective surgery. RP is also an option for highly selected patients with local recurrence after EBRT, brachytherapy, or cryotherapy. RP should be viewed as one component of a multimodality approach for high-risk disease [PROS-K].
principles: RP is appropriate for any patient not on active surveillance with clinically localized prostate cancer that can be completely excised, life expectancy ≥10 years, and no serious comorbidities [PROS-K]
High-volume surgeons in high-volume centers generally provide better outcomes [PROS-K]
Blood loss can be reduced by laparoscopic or robotic assistance [PROS-K]
Urinary incontinence reduced by preserving urethral length beyond apex and avoiding distal sphincter damage; bladder neck preservation may decrease incontinence risk [PROS-K]
Erectile function recovery related to age, preoperative function, and degree of cavernous nerve preservation; nerve grafts not shown beneficial [PROS-K]

Extended PLND recommended when PLND is performed, including removal of node-bearing tissue bounded by external iliac vein, pelvic sidewall, bladder wall, pelvis floor, Cooper's ligament, and internal iliac artery [PROS-K]
RP + PLND can be considered in favorable intermediate through very-high risk; recommended in unfavorable intermediate, high, very-high, and regional disease [PROS-K]

procedures.name: Radical Prostatectomy
procedures.approach: Open retropubic, laparoscopic, or robot-assisted radical prostatectomy
procedures.indications: Clinically localized prostate cancer (N0, M0) with life expectancy ≥10 years; RP should be viewed as one component of multimodality approach for high-risk disease; select patients with N1 disease with >10-year life expectancy and resectable disease [PROS-7A, PROS-K]
