procedures.key_complications: Urinary incontinence (20%-50% requiring ≥1 pad/day), erectile dysfunction (40%-60% risk), penile shortening, urethral/bladder neck contracture or stricture [PROS-K]
procedures.operative_mortality: Low with experienced surgeons; blood loss reduced with minimally invasive approaches

procedures.name: Pelvic Lymph Node Dissection (PLND)
procedures.indications: Recommended in unfavorable intermediate, high, very-high, and regional prostate cancer. Can be considered in favorable intermediate-risk. Extended PLND recommended when performed [PROS-K]
procedures.key_complications: Lymphocele, deep vein thrombosis, lymphedema; complication rates similar across open, laparoscopic, and robotic approaches [PROS-K]

procedures.name: Secondary Radical Prostatectomy
procedures.approach: Open or minimally invasive by experienced surgeons
procedures.indications: Highly selected patients with local recurrence after EBRT, brachytherapy, or cryotherapy without metastatic disease; morbidity high (incontinence, loss of erection, anastomotic stricture, rectal injury) [PROS-K]
procedures.key_complications: High rates of urinary incontinence, erectile dysfunction, anastomotic stricture, rectal injury [PROS-K]

surgery.resectability_criteria: RP is appropriate for patients with clinically localized prostate cancer that can be completely excised surgically, life expectancy ≥10 years, and no serious comorbidities contraindicating elective surgery [PROS-K]. PLND can be excluded in patients with low predicted probability of nodal metastases by nomograms (published thresholds 2%-7%), but a negative PSMA PET should not preclude PLND due to low sensitivity (~40%) and NPV (~81%) for pelvic lymph node involvement [PROS-K]. RP should NOT be routinely used for very-high-risk, regional, or metastatic disease outside of clinical trials or when RT+ADT is contraindicated [PROS-K].
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: No specific pre-operative optimization protocols described in the guidelines beyond standard surgical assessment. Assessment of life expectancy, comorbidities, and shared decision-making are essential prior to surgery. Assessment of baseline urinary, sexual, and bowel function using standardized instruments is recommended [PROS-D].

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy (RT) is a primary treatment option for clinically localized prostate cancer across all risk groups. RT is also used in combination with ADT for regional (N1M0) disease, in the post-prostatectomy setting for adverse features or biochemical recurrence, for metastasis-directed therapy, and as palliative treatment. IMRT is recommended over 3D conformal RT. Image guidance is encouraged and may include CBCT or MRI [PROS-J].
principles: IMRT recommended over 3D conformal RT to improve dose conformality [PROS-J]
Image guidance with daily 3D imaging (CBCT or MRI) recommended; devices for motion management (fiducials, endorectal balloons) encouraged [PROS-J]
Photon and proton RT are acceptable and appear to have similar outcomes; potential financial toxicity should be discussed [PROS-J]
Conventionally fractionated RT is no longer preferred for localized prostate cancer; moderate hypofractionation and ultra-hypofractionation are noninferior [PROS-J]
SBRT preferred specifically for MDRT, oligoprogression, re-irradiation, and at physician discretion for more durable pain control [PROS-J]
Biocompatible perirectal spacers may be implanted to reduce rectal dose; patients with gross posterior extraprostatic extension should NOT undergo spacer implantation [PROS-J]

dose_frameworks.name: Moderate Hypofractionation (Preferred)
dose_frameworks.indication: Preferred for low, FIR, UIR, high, very-high risk localized disease; also used for sRT and post-adjuvant settings

dose_frameworks.name: SBRT
dose_frameworks.schedule: Daily or every other day over 1-2 weeks
dose_frameworks.indication: Localized prostate (UIR, high-risk with caution); MDRT; oligoprogressive disease

dose_frameworks.name: Ultra-Hypofractionation (SBRT-adjacent)
dose_frameworks.indication: Localized prostate with image guidance expertise

dose_frameworks.name: Conventional Fractionation
dose_frameworks.indication: Less preferred but acceptable; no longer preferred for localized disease

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative RT
dose_frameworks.indication: Bone metastases pain palliation; equivalent to longer courses for short-term pain control but higher re-treatment rates

approaches.name: EBRT for Low-Risk Disease
approaches.dose_fractionation: Moderate hypofractionation (60 Gy in 20 fx) or SBRT (35-40 Gy in 5 fx) preferred
approaches.indication: Low-risk disease if treatment elected (active surveillance preferred)
approaches.toxicities: GI: bowel symptoms; GU: urinary symptoms; Sexual: erectile dysfunction

approaches.name: EBRT + Short-Term ADT for Unfavorable Intermediate-Risk
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: ST-ADT (4-6 months) concurrent/adjuvant; NOT with neoadjuvant
approaches.toxicities: ADT-related: hot flashes, fatigue, sexual dysfunction, bone loss, metabolic changes; RT-related: GI and GU toxicity

approaches.name: EBRT + Long-Term ADT for High/Very-High Risk
approaches.dose_fractionation: Moderate hypofractionation; SBRT may be considered
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: LT-ADT (12-36 months); for high-risk with brachytherapy boost, 12 months may be considered
approaches.indication: High-risk or very-high-risk localized prostate cancer
approaches.toxicities: ADT-related: hot flashes, fatigue, sexual dysfunction, bone loss, metabolic changes, cardiovascular risk; RT-related: GI and GU toxicity; Brachytherapy boost: higher GU toxicity (grade 3: 18.4% at 5 years)

approaches.name: EBRT + ADT + Abiraterone for N1 Disease
approaches.dose_fractionation: EBRT including prostate, seminal vesicles, and pelvic lymph nodes; simultaneous integrated boost to involved nodes
approaches.toxicities: Combined ADT, abiraterone, and RT toxicities; grade 3-5 AE with abiraterone: 47% vs 33% (STAMPEDE)

approaches.name: EBRT for Synchronous Low-Volume M1 Disease
approaches.dose_fractionation: EBRT to primary tumor (55 Gy in 20 fx or 36 Gy in 6 weekly fx)
approaches.indication: Low-volume synchronous mCSPC (non-regional lymph-node-only OR <4 bone metastases without visceral disease)
approaches.key_trial: STAMPEDE (OS benefit in low-volume: HR 0.68), PEACE-1
approaches.toxicities: Reduced severe genitourinary adverse events with RT to primary (PEACE-1)

approaches.name: Brachytherapy Monotherapy
approaches.dose_fractionation: LDR: I-125 145 Gy, Pd-103 125 Gy, Cs-131 115 Gy permanent seed implant; HDR: Ir-192 13.5 Gy x 2 implants or 9.5 Gy BID x 2 implants
approaches.indication: Low-risk, FIR, carefully selected UIR localized disease
approaches.key_trial: NRG/RTOG 0232 (brachytherapy alone vs brachytherapy + EBRT for intermediate risk)
approaches.toxicities: Urinary frequency, urgency, rectal pain; erectile dysfunction lower with HDR than LDR

approaches.name: EBRT + Brachytherapy Boost
approaches.dose_fractionation: EBRT 45-50.4 Gy in 25-28 fx OR 37.5 Gy in 15 fx + LDR boost (I-125: 110-115 Gy, Pd-103: 90-100 Gy, Cs-131: 85 Gy) or HDR boost (Ir-192: 15 Gy x 1 fx or 10.75 Gy x 2 fx)
approaches.indication: UIR, high-risk, carefully selected very-high-risk localized disease
approaches.key_trial: ASCENDE-RT (improved bPFS with LDR boost: 86% vs 75% at 7 years; higher GU toxicity)
approaches.toxicities: Increased grade 3 GU events (18.4% at 5 years); increased GI toxicity trend (8.1% vs 3.2% at 5 years)

approaches.name: Radiation for Bone Metastases (MDT/SBRT)
approaches.dose_fractionation: SBRT: 9-10 Gy x 3 fx, 12 Gy x 2 fx, 16-24 Gy x 1 fx; or moderate hypofractionation 6.2-6.4 Gy x 5 fx
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Concurrent systemic therapy (ADT+ARPI for mCSPC; mCRPC systemic therapy)
approaches.indication: Metastasis-directed therapy for oligometastatic CSPC and mCRPC; palliative RT for painful bone metastases
approaches.key_trial: ORIOLE, STOMP, EXTEND, RADIOSA (CSPC); ARTO, GROUQ-PCS-9 (mCRPC)
approaches.toxicities: Generally well-tolerated with SBRT; rare toxicities based on treatment site

approaches.name: SRT with ADT After RP
approaches.dose_fractionation: 52.5 Gy in 20 fx (moderate hypofractionation preferred) or 64-70.2 Gy in 32-39 fx
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: ST-ADT (6 months) generally recommended; LT-ADT for late sRT, multiple adverse features, or N1 disease
approaches.indication: PSA persistence/recurrence after RP without M1 disease; early sRT at PSA 0.1-0.2 ng/mL recommended
approaches.key_trial: RADICALS-RT, RTOG 9601, GETUG-AFU 16, RADICALS-HD, SPPORT, DADSPORT meta-analysis
approaches.toxicities: Genitourinary and gastrointestinal toxicity; urinary incontinence and urethral strictures more frequent with adjuvant therapy

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy options for prostate cancer include androgen deprivation therapy (ADT), androgen receptor pathway inhibitors (ARPIs), chemotherapy, immunotherapy, PARP inhibitors, radiopharmaceuticals, and targeted agents. ADT forms the backbone of treatment for metastatic and recurrent disease. Treatment intensification with ARPIs and/or docetaxel is strongly recommended for mCSPC. For mCRPC, treatment options are organized by prior ARPI and docetaxel exposure. Biomarker-directed therapy (PARP inhibitors for HRR mutations, pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR) and radiopharmaceuticals (Lu-177-PSMA-617, radium-223) have expanded the therapeutic landscape. FDA-approved biosimilar agents are appropriate substitutes for recommended biologic therapies [PROS-N].

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Neoadjuvant/Concurrent/Adjuvant ADT with RT for Localized Disease
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: LHRH agonist or antagonist monotherapy or with first-generation antiandrogen; for very-high-risk or N1: plus abiraterone (2 years) [PROS-G]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: ST-ADT (4-6 months) for UIR; LT-ADT (18-36 months) for high/very-high risk. Concurrent/adjuvant ADT preferred over neoadjuvant [PROS-G]

by_stage_and_setting.setting: M0 CSPC (Regional N1, M0; First M0 RP or RT recurrence)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Orchiectomy, LHRH agonist, or LHRH antagonist ± first-generation antiandrogen; for N1: plus abiraterone [PROS-G]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: ADT for regional disease or M0 recurrent disease
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: ADT with abiraterone for N1 only [PROS-G]

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Monitoring (preferred for low-risk BCR2); ADT for high-risk BCR2; enzalutamide ± leuprolide or apalutamide + ADT for high-risk BCR2 [PROS-12]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Monitoring preferred for low-risk BCR2; treatment intensification for high-risk BCR2 based on EMBARK and PRESTO trials

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: ADT + abiraterone, apalutamide, or enzalutamide (category 1); or ADT + darolutamide (category 2B) [PROS-14]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Doublet therapy with ADT + ARPI; triplet therapy with ADT + docetaxel + ARPI for fit patients (category 2B); EBRT to primary tumor for synchronous low-volume [PROS-14]
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Triplet therapy; ADT + niraparib/abiraterone for BRCA2m; ADT + EBRT to primary ± ARPI or docetaxel

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: ADT + docetaxel + abiraterone (category 1) or ADT + docetaxel + darolutamide (category 1) [PROS-15]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Triplet therapy preferred based on PEACE-1 and ARASENS; doublet therapy also appropriate
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: ADT + ARPI doublet; ADT + niraparib/abiraterone for BRCA2m

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Monitoring for PSADT >10 months (preferred); Apalutamide (cat1), Darolutamide (cat1), or Enzalutamide (cat1) for PSADT ≤10 months [PROS-16]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Secondary hormone therapy to delay metastases
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Other secondary hormone therapies (abiraterone, enzalutamide following ARPI)
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: PSADT assessment; document castrate testosterone levels

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: First-line ARPI for mCRPC without prior ARPI exposure
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Docetaxel (cat1); PARP inhibitor combinations for BRCAm/HRRm
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: HRR gene mutations for PARP inhibitors; MSI-H/dMMR for pembrolizumab; TMB testing; PSMA-PET for Lu-177-PSMA-617 eligibility

by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: PARP inhibitors (olaparib/cat1, rucaparib/cat1 preferred for BRCAm); Lu-177-PSMA-617 for PSMA-positive; cabazitaxel/carboplatin for aggressive variant
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: BRCA/HRR mutations; PSMA status; aggressive variant criteria

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: After progression on both ARPI and docetaxel
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: PARP inhibitors (olaparib/cat1 for BRCAm); Lu-177-PSMA-617 (cat1 for PSMA-positive); cabazitaxel/carboplatin; mitoxantrone for palliation

by_stage_and_setting.setting: M1 CRPC - Additional Options (Any Prior Therapy)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Sipuleucel-T for asymptomatic without visceral metastases; Pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR (cat2B); MDT for oligometastatic/oligoprogressive; Radium-223 for symptomatic bone metastases without visceral disease (cat1) [PROS-18]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Options available regardless of prior therapy based on disease state and biomarkers
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: MSI-H/dMMR for pembrolizumab; ECOG 0-1 for sipuleucel-T; no visceral metastases for radium-223 and sipuleucel-T

key_regimens.name: Abiraterone + Prednisone + ADT
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hypertension (22%), hypokalemia (17%), peripheral edema (28%), fatigue (39%), hepatotoxicity (grade 3-5: 7%), cardiac events

key_regimens.name: Apalutamide + ADT
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Rash (24%), hypothyroidism (8%), fatigue, fracture (11% in M0 CRPC)

key_regimens.name: Enzalutamide + ADT
key_regimens.setting: mCSPC (ENZAMET, ARCHES); M0 CRPC (PROSPER); mCRPC (PREVAIL, AFFIRM)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fatigue (33%), hypertension (12%), seizures (0.9%), mental impairment disorders (5%), major adverse cardiovascular events (5%)

key_regimens.name: Darolutamide + ADT
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Fatigue (12.1%), pain in extremity (5.8%), rash (2.9%); fracture rate similar to placebo (4.2% vs 3.6%)

key_regimens.name: ADT + Docetaxel + Abiraterone (Triplet)
key_regimens.cycle_days: Docetaxel 6 cycles, then abiraterone maintenance until progression
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade ≥3 AE: 63% (triplet) vs 52% (ADT+docetaxel); neutropenia, febrile neutropenia, fatigue, neuropathy similar between triplet and doublet

key_regimens.name: ADT + Docetaxel + Darolutamide (Triplet)
key_regimens.cycle_days: Docetaxel 6 cycles, then darolutamide maintenance until progression
key_regimens.key_toxicities: AE profile similar between arms; most common: alopecia (40.5%), neutropenia (39.3%), fatigue (33.1%), anemia (27.8%); rash (16.6% vs 13.5%) and hypertension (13.7% vs 9.2%) more common with darolutamide

key_regimens.name: Niraparib + Abiraterone + ADT
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade 3/4 AE: 75% vs 59% (control); anemia (29.1% vs 4.6%), hypertension (26.5% vs 18.4%); treatment-emergent AE leading to death: 14 vs 7 participants

key_regimens.name: Docetaxel
key_regimens.cycle_days: Every 3 weeks; up to 10 cycles in mCRPC pivotal trials; duration based on benefit/toxicity
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia, febrile neutropenia (14% with 3-weekly), neuropathy, fluid retention, hypersensitivity, hepatic function impairment, fatigue, alopecia

key_regimens.name: Cabazitaxel
key_regimens.cycle_days: Every 3 weeks; growth factor support recommended
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Febrile neutropenia, severe diarrhea (6%), fatigue (5%), anemia (11%), thrombocytopenia (4%); lower neuropathy than docetaxel at 20 mg/m2 (12% vs 25%)

key_regimens.name: Olaparib
key_regimens.setting: mCRPC with BRCA1/2 or HRR mutations (PROfound)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Anemia (including transfusion-required), fatigue, nausea/vomiting, thrombocytopenia, creatinine elevation; rare: thromboembolic events, pneumonitis, theoretical MDS/AML risk

key_regimens.name: Rucaparib
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Anemia (grade ≥3: 25.2%), fatigue, nausea, thrombocytopenia, theoretical MDS/AML risk

key_regimens.name: Olaparib + Abiraterone
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Anemia, fatigue/asthenia, nausea; HR for rPFS: 0.66 (ITT), 0.50 (HRRm); final OS not significantly improved in full cohort (HR 0.81, P=.054)

key_regimens.name: Talazoparib + Enzalutamide
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Anemia (31%), neutropenia (8%), thrombocytopenia (9%); dose interruption 75% (talazoparib) vs 23% (placebo); dose reduction 56% vs 7%

key_regimens.name: Lu-177-PSMA-617
key_regimens.setting: mCRPC with PSMA-positive metastases post-ARPI (VISION); also pre-docetaxel (PSMAfore)
key_regimens.cycle_days: Every 6 weeks for up to 6 treatments
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade ≥3: anemia, thrombocytopenia, lymphopenia, fatigue; not recommended if dominant PSMA-negative lesions

key_regimens.name: Radium-223
key_regimens.setting: mCRPC with symptomatic bone metastases, no visceral disease (ALSYMPCA); with enzalutamide (PEACE-3)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Low hematologic toxicity (3% neutropenia, 6% thrombocytopenia, 13% anemia); nausea, diarrhea, vomiting; concomitant denosumab or zoledronic acid recommended to reduce fracture risk; should NOT be used with visceral metastases

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab
key_regimens.setting: mCRPC with MSI-H/dMMR (tumor-agnostic); consider TMB ≥10 mut/mB
key_regimens.cycle_days: Every 3 weeks; continue up to 2 years or until progression/unacceptable toxicity
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-mediated: pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, myocarditis, endocrinopathies, exfoliative dermatologic conditions, nephritis, ocular toxicities

key_regimens.name: Sipuleucel-T
key_regimens.setting: Asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic mCRPC without visceral metastases
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Chills (54.1%), pyrexia (29.3%), headache (16.0%); median OS 25.8 vs 21.7 months (HR 0.78); ECOG 0-1, life expectancy >6 months, no liver metastases, no visceral metastases

treatment_response_assessment.title: Monitoring and Treatment Response Assessment
treatment_response_assessment.timing: After initial definitive therapy: PSA every 6-12 months for 5 years, then annually. PSA as frequently as every 3 months may be needed for high-risk patients. After definitive RT: consider DRE if suspicion of recurrence [PROS-8].
response_logic: PSA recurrence after RP: defined as PSA that does not fall to undetectable (persistence) or undetectable PSA followed by detectable PSA increasing on ≥2 determinations or PSA >0.1 ng/mL [PROS-8A footnote w]

PSA recurrence after RT: PSA increase ≥2 ng/mL above nadir (Phoenix definition); recurrence evaluation considered when PSA confirmed increasing even if <2 ng/mL above nadir, especially for young/healthy candidates for secondary therapy [PROS-8A footnote v]
PSADT calculation required to inform nomogram use and counseling [PROS-10A footnote mm]
Document castrate levels of testosterone (<50 ng/dL) if progression occurs on ADT; workup for progression should include bone and soft tissue evaluation [PROS-8A footnote ff]
Increasing PSA should NOT be used as sole criteria for progression in mCRPC; assessment should incorporate clinical and radiographic criteria [PROS-N]
imaging_recommendations: PSA every 6-12 months for 5 years after initial definitive therapy, then annually [PROS-8]
For post-RP patients: imaging for symptoms or increasing PSA; bone scan rarely positive at PSA <10 ng/mL; PSMA-PET may detect disease at PSA 0.2-0.5 ng/mL [PROS-9]
For N1M0 on ADT: physical exam + PSA every 3-6 months; imaging for symptoms or increasing PSA [PROS-8]
For localized disease on observation: same monitoring as N1M0 on ADT [PROS-8]
Bone scans should be performed for symptoms and as often as every 6-12 months to monitor ADT; in CRPC, 8-12 week intervals appear reasonable [PROS-E]
Imaging for progressive CRPC should include chest CT, bone imaging, and abdomen/pelvis CT with contrast or MRI [PROS-E]
New lesions on bone scan in setting of falling PSA or soft tissue response may indicate bone scan flare; confirmatory bone scan 8-12 weeks later warranted [PROS-E]

PSMA-PET imaging should only be used in M1 CRPC setting to determine candidacy for Lu-177-PSMA-617; changes in systemic therapy should NOT be made solely based on positive PSMA-PET in M0 CRPC [PROS-E]
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Prostate bed biopsy can be considered in patients with PSA persistence/recurrence after RP if life expectancy >5 years and considering local therapy [PROS-9]
Prostate/seminal vesicle biopsy should be considered after RT if local recurrence suspected and staging workup does not reveal metastatic disease [PROS-10]
Metastatic biopsy strongly recommended for histologic and molecular evaluation in mCRPC; plasma ctDNA assay if biopsy unsafe/unfeasible [PROS-C, PROS-17]
Biopsy should be performed to confirm residual/recurrent prostate cancer after focal ablative therapy [PROS-I]
Rapid PSA increase may warrant evaluation (prostate biopsy) prior to meeting Phoenix definition, especially in younger/healthy patients [PROS-8A footnote v]

prognosis.overall: For all stages combined, the 5-year relative survival rate for prostate cancer is 97% [1]. The comparatively low death rate suggests that increased public awareness with earlier detection and treatment has affected mortality from this prevalent cancer, but is also complicated by screening-related lead-time bias and detection of indolent cancers. Researchers estimate that prostate cancer will account for 11% of male cancer deaths in the United States in 2025, with an estimated 35,770 deaths [1]. The age-adjusted death rate from prostate cancer declined by 52% from 1993 to 2017, but the death rate has become more stable in recent years, with a 0.5% annual decrease from 2012 through 2022 [1]. Large inequities exist in incidence and mortality: the incidence rate in Black individuals is 67% higher than in white individuals, and the mortality rate is two to four times higher than all other racial and ethnic groups [11]. However, overall prognosis by race appears similar when patients are treated with the same guideline-concordant care [12].

by_stage.context: Very low risk of prostate cancer-specific mortality; active surveillance is preferred for most patients with low-risk disease and life expectancy ≥10 years
Low risk; active surveillance preferred, though definitive local therapy with RT or RP is appropriate for those with longer life expectancy desiring treatment
Favorable intermediate-risk disease; active surveillance may be considered for select patients; RT without ADT or RP appropriate options
Unfavorable intermediate-risk disease; RT with short-term ADT (4-6 months) or RP are recommended; active surveillance not recommended for patients with life expectancy >10 years
High-risk disease; RT with long-term ADT (18-36 months) or RP plus PLND; active surveillance not recommended
High-risk disease with extraprostatic extension; RT with long-term ADT or multi-modality approach including RP in select patients
Very-high-risk disease (Grade Group 5); RT with long-term ADT (18-36 months); addition of abiraterone for select very-high-risk patients
Regional disease; RT with long-term ADT plus abiraterone (preferred), or ADT alone plus abiraterone; 5-year survival significantly lower than localized disease

Metastatic disease; ADT with treatment intensification strongly recommended; low-volume synchronous oligometastatic disease may benefit from EBRT to primary tumor; prognosis varies significantly by volume and site of metastases

prognostic_factors: NCCN risk group stratification based on T stage, Grade Group (Gleason score), and PSA level
PSA doubling time (PSADT): shorter PSADT (<12 months) associated with higher risk of progression and death from prostate cancer
Volume of metastatic disease (CHAARTED criteria: high-volume defined by visceral metastasis or ≥4 bone lesions with ≥1 beyond vertebral bodies and pelvis)
Gleason grade and pattern 4 percentage: Grade Group 3 (4+3) has higher risk than Grade Group 2 (3+4)
Intraductal and cribriform histology: associated with poor prognosis and increased risk of metastases
Germline mutations (BRCA2, ATM, CHEK2): associated with more aggressive phenotype and significantly reduced survival times
Age and comorbidities: affect life expectancy estimation and treatment recommendation
PSA density, number of positive cores, and percentage of positive cores
Adverse pathologic features (post-surgical): positive margins, seminal vesicle invasion, extracapsular extension
22-gene genomic classifier (Decipher): independently prognostic for distant metastasis, prostate cancer-specific mortality, and overall survival in multiple randomized trials
Multimodal AI (MMAI) biomarker: independently prognostic for distant metastasis, PCSM, and death after DM in high- and very-high-risk disease
Race/ethnicity: Black individuals have higher incidence and mortality, though outcomes appear similar with equitable treatment
prognosis.prognostic_context: Prostate cancer prognosis is highly dependent on disease stage at diagnosis, with near-universal 5-year survival for localized disease but substantially lower survival for metastatic disease. The heterogeneity within NCCN risk groups means that additional prognostic tools (genomic classifiers, digital pathology-based AI tools, nomograms) can help personalize treatment decisions. Life expectancy estimation is critical because many prostate cancer patients have competing comorbidities, and the benefits of treatment must be weighed against potential harms, especially given the long natural history of the disease.

disease_related.management: Denosumab 120 mg SQ every 4 weeks (category 1, preferred) or zoledronic acid 4 mg IV every 12 weeks for prevention of symptomatic SREs in patients with bone-metastatic CRPC. EBRT for palliation of painful bone metastases. Radium-223 for symptomatic bone metastases without visceral metastases. Bisphosphonates or denosumab for bone loss prevention in castration-sensitive patients on ADT based on FRAX risk assessment.
Emergency MRI for suspected spinal cord compression; emergent decompressive surgery if feasible followed by RT, or RT alone. EBRT can be considered for patients with limited life expectancy.
Surgical fixation as needed; antiresorptive therapy with denosumab or zoledronic acid for prevention; calcium and vitamin D supplementation; evaluation of fall risk; weight-bearing exercises.
TURP for bladder outlet obstruction; nephrostomy tubes or ureteral stents for ureteral obstruction; palliative RT to prostate if appropriate.
Systemic therapy based on prior treatments and biomarker status; palliative RT for symptom control; chemotherapy with platinum-based regimens if neuroendocrine features present.

PSA monitoring per post-treatment protocols; secondary therapy options include RT (for post-RP recurrence), local secondary therapy including cryotherapy, HIFU, IRE, reirradiation, or RP (for post-RT recurrence); systemic therapy if metastatic disease present.

disease_related.complication: Progression to neuroendocrine/small cell prostate cancer
disease_related.management: Biopsy of metastatic lesions to identify small cell/neuroendocrine histology; cytotoxic chemotherapy with cisplatin/etoposide, carboplatin/etoposide, docetaxel/carboplatin, or cabazitaxel/carboplatin; consult NCCN Guidelines for Small Cell Lung Cancer for additional options.

chemotherapy: Docetaxel: neutropenia, leukopenia, febrile neutropenia, neutropenic infections, fluid retention, hypersensitivity reaction, hepatic function impairment, neuropathy, fatigue, nausea, vomiting, alopecia, diarrhea

Cabazitaxel: febrile neutropenia, severe diarrhea (6%), fatigue (5%), nausea/vomiting, anemia (11%), thrombocytopenia (4%), sepsis, renal failure; lower neuropathy than docetaxel especially at 20 mg/m2
Mitoxantrone: similar adverse events to docetaxel but lower rates of grade 3-4 neutropenic fevers, cardiovascular events, nausea and vomiting, metabolic disturbances, and neurologic events
immunotherapy: Sipuleucel-T: mild to moderate chills (54.1%), pyrexia (29.3%), headache (16.0%); usually transient; not recommended if visceral metastases present

Pembrolizumab: severe immune-mediated adverse reactions including pneumonitis, colitis, hepatitis, myocarditis, endocrinopathies, exfoliative dermatologic conditions, renal failure, nephritis, ocular toxicities
targeted_therapy: PARP inhibitors (olaparib, rucaparib, niraparib, talazoparib): anemia (including requiring transfusion), fatigue, nausea, vomiting, anorexia, weight loss, diarrhea, thrombocytopenia, creatinine elevation; rare but serious: thromboembolic events, drug-induced pneumonitis, theoretical risk of myelodysplasia or acute myeloid leukemia
PARP inhibitor combinations with ARPIs: higher rates of anemia (29% with niraparib/abiraterone vs 4.6% control), hypertension, and grade 3-4 adverse events (75% vs 59% for niraparib arm)
Abiraterone: hypertension, hypokalemia, edema, fatigue, hot flushes, liver toxicity, cardiac events (rare atrial fibrillation)
Darolutamide: fatigue, pain in extremity, rash
surgery: Radical prostatectomy: urinary incontinence (20-50% requiring ≥1 pad/day), erectile dysfunction (40-60%), penile shortening, urethral or bladder neck contracture/stricture; anastomotic strictures increase risk of long-term incontinence
Pelvic lymph node dissection: lymphocele, lymphedema, deep vein thrombosis, vascular injury
Secondary RP after RT: significantly higher morbidity including incontinence, erectile dysfunction, bladder neck contracture, and rectal injury
radiation: External beam RT: acute GI toxicity (rectal urgency, frequency, diarrhea), acute GU toxicity (urinary frequency, urgency, dysuria); late GI toxicity (rectal bleeding, fistula); late GU toxicity (urethral stricture, hematuria)
Brachytherapy: urinary frequency, urgency, retention; erectile dysfunction; rectal injury; urethral stricture
SBRT: generally well-tolerated; urinary, GI, and sexual side effects similar to conventional fractionation

Radium-223: nausea, diarrhea, vomiting (generally mild due to fecal elimination); low hematologic toxicity (3% neutropenia, 6% thrombocytopenia, 13% anemia); fracture risk increased when combined with abiraterone without bone-protecting agents

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care is an integral component of prostate cancer management throughout the disease continuum. The NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship provide comprehensive recommendations regarding common consequences of cancer and cancer treatment, including anxiety, depression, trauma, distress, hormone-related symptoms, sexual dysfunction, and promotion of physical activity, weight management, and immunizations. Shared decision-making incorporating quality-of-life assessments using validated instruments (e.g., EPIC-26) is essential. Geriatric assessment should be considered for older adults. The NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List, which includes social determinants of health, should be utilized.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Calcium supplementation of 1000-1200 mg daily from food with supplements if intake is insufficient is recommended for all patients receiving ADT. Vitamin D3 supplementation to maintain serum levels of 30-50 ng/mL is recommended. Nutritional assessment should be performed as part of comprehensive care, particularly for patients experiencing weight loss, anorexia, or treatment-related gastrointestinal side effects.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: For chemotherapy-related nausea and vomiting, antiemetic prophylaxis should follow current ASCO/ONS antiemetic guidelines. For abiraterone/prednisone therapy, antiemetics are generally not routinely required. Dexamethasone-based regimens (e.g., with docetaxel) provide inherent antiemetic protection. Supportive antiemetic agents should be provided for patients receiving taxane-based chemotherapy.
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: Growth factor support should follow NCCN Guidelines for Hematopoietic Growth Factors based on risk of neutropenic fever. Prophylactic G-CSF is recommended for patients ≥65 years receiving biweekly cabazitaxel at 16 mg/m2 dose. The use of myeloid growth factors should follow risk assessment for each chemotherapy regimen. For docetaxel every 3 weeks, G-CSF should be considered based on individual patient risk factors.
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Venous thromboembolism prophylaxis should be considered for patients receiving PARP inhibitors, particularly those with additional risk factors. ADT is associated with increased risk of ischemic and thrombotic events, and patients should be monitored for these complications. Patients with prior VTE history on PARP inhibitor therapy warrant close monitoring and may require prophylactic anticoagulation.
supportive_care.pain_management: Pain management follows NCCN Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain. Options include pharmacologic management (NSAIDs, opioids, adjuvant analgesics), RT for painful bone metastases (8 Gy x 1 fraction is as effective as longer courses for short-term palliation), and interventional procedures. Radium-223 improves time to pain progression in patients with bone-metastatic CRPC without visceral metastases. Bone antiresorptive therapy with denosumab or zoledronic acid reduces skeletal-related events.
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Patient-reported outcomes should be measured using standardized instruments such as EPIC-26 for urinary, bowel, and sexual function assessment. Shared decision-making in a multidisciplinary manner should include explanation of potential benefits and harms of each option. The NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List should be used to assess and address distress, including social determinants of health. Studies indicate that patient anxiety during active surveillance is closely linked with provider education on safety of active surveillance.
supportive_care.dental_care: A comprehensive dental evaluation is recommended for all patients before initiating an osteoclast inhibitor (bisphosphonate or denosumab) to prevent osteonecrosis of the jaw. If invasive dental procedures are required, bone-targeted therapy should be withheld until the dentist confirms complete healing from all dental procedures. Oral hygiene, baseline dental evaluation, and avoidance of invasive dental surgery during therapy are recommended to reduce ONJ risk. Stopping denosumab represents a dilemma as discontinuation can result in rebound bone loss and fractures.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: For patients initially treated with curative intent, serum PSA levels should be measured every 6 to 12 months for the first 5 years and then annually [PROS-8]. PSA testing every 3 months may be necessary to clarify disease status, especially in patients at high risk of recurrence [PROS-8A footnote ee]. When prostate cancer recurred after radical prostatectomy, Pound and colleagues found that 45% of patients experienced recurrence within the first 2 years, 77% within the first 5 years, and 96% by 10 years [431]. Post-RP PSA persistence/recurrence is defined as when PSA does not fall to undetectable levels (persistence) or undetectable PSA after RP with a subsequent detectable PSA that increases on ≥2 determinations or increases to PSA >0.1 ng/mL [PROS-9A]. Post-RT biochemical recurrence is defined per the Phoenix Consensus as PSA increase by ≥2 ng/mL above the nadir PSA, or a confirmed increasing PSA after radiation even if the increase above nadir is <2 ng/mL [PROS-7A footnote v].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: The rationale for surveillance after definitive therapy is to detect recurrence at the earliest possible time when secondary treatment is most likely to be effective. Early secondary treatment (at a PSA of 0.1-0.2 ng/mL) is recommended for most patients who experience a biochemical recurrence after RP, as it is associated with decreased all-cause mortality and increased prostate cancer-specific survival [451,452]. The utility of imaging for patients with early PSA persistence/recurrence after RP depends on risk group prior to operation, pathologic Gleason grade and stage, PSA, and PSADT after recurrence. PSMA-PET/CT has increased sensitivity at low PSA levels post-RP, with approximately 30-40% of patients having detectable disease at PSA levels of 0.2 to 0.5 ng/mL.
late_effects_screening: Bone health: DEXA scan before starting ADT in patients at increased risk based on FRAX screening; repeat DEXA every 1-2 years while on ADT or antiresorptive therapy; fracture risk assessment using FRAX annually

Cardiovascular disease: assessment for pre-existing and emerging CVD; risk factor management including hypertension, hyperlipidemia, diabetes, obesity; screening for diabetes and cardiovascular disease for patients receiving ADT
Metabolic effects of ADT: monitoring for obesity, insulin resistance, lipid alterations, diabetes
Cognitive function: monitoring for cognitive effects, particularly with second-generation ARPIs
Psychosocial: assessment for depression, anxiety, erectile dysfunction-related psychological effects
Urinary and sexual function: ongoing assessment using validated patient-reported outcomes instruments
Gonadal function recovery: monitoring of testosterone levels after ADT cessation
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: PSA recurrence after RP typically occurs within the first 5 years in the majority of patients (77%), with 96% experiencing recurrence by 10 years. The pattern of recurrence is influenced by initial disease risk: patients with lower risk have later and slower recurrences. PSADT is a critical prognostic factor; shorter PSADT (<12 months) indicates more aggressive disease and higher risk of metastatic progression. Local recurrence after RT is most responsive to additional therapy when PSA levels at the time of treatment are low (<5 ng/mL). Bone scan flare is common particularly on initiation of new hormonal therapy, and may be observed in nearly half of patients treated with enzalutamide and abiraterone.

clinical_follow_up_schedule.setting: Post-definitive therapy (RP or RT) - first 5 years
clinical_follow_up_schedule.schedule: PSA every 6-12 months; consider DRE if suspicion of recurrence
PSA annually; consider DRE if suspicion of recurrence
PSA as frequently as every 3 months may be necessary to clarify disease status
Physical examination + PSA every 3-6 months; imaging for symptoms or increasing PSA
Physical examination + PSA every 3-6 months; imaging for symptoms or increasing PSA
PSA no more often than every 6 months; DRE no more often than every 12 months; repeat prostate biopsy no more often than every 12 months; repeat mpMRI no more than every 12 months

Same as low risk but with greater intensity of monitoring; confirmatory prostate biopsy within 1-2 years of diagnostic biopsy; consider earlier repeat biopsy within 6-12 months if initial biopsy was not mpMRI-guided
Monitoring with physical exam, PSA every 3-6 months, and imaging for symptoms or increasing PSA; document castrate levels of testosterone

clinical_follow_up_schedule.setting: M1 CRPC or mCSPC on systemic therapy
clinical_follow_up_schedule.schedule: Physical examination + PSA every 3-6 months; periodic imaging to monitor treatment response; in CRPC, 8-12 week imaging intervals appear reasonable

imaging_strategy.imaging: No routine imaging recommended; bone and soft tissue imaging not indicated for asymptomatic patients with PSA <10 ng/mL

imaging_strategy.setting: Unfavorable intermediate-risk, high-risk, very-high-risk localized disease
imaging_strategy.imaging: Bone and soft tissue imaging recommended; PSMA-PET/CT or PSMA-PET/MRI can be considered as alternative to CT, MRI, and bone scans; mpMRI preferred over CT for pelvic staging
Bone and soft tissue imaging for life expectancy >5 years; PSMA-PET/CT has increased sensitivity at low PSA levels; bone scans rarely positive in asymptomatic patients with PSA <10 ng/mL
PSADT calculation, bone and soft tissue imaging, consideration of prostate/seminal vesicle biopsy if local recurrence suspected
Bone scans and soft tissue imaging obtained regularly during systemic therapy; bone scans as often as every 6-12 months for ADT monitoring; 8-12 week imaging intervals in CRPC
PSMA-PET imaging should only be used to determine if patient is a candidate for Lu-177-PSMA-617; changes in systemic therapy should not be made solely based on positive PSMA-PET in M0 CRPC

laboratory_monitoring: PSA: cornerstone of monitoring; frequency depends on disease state and risk of recurrence
PSA doubling time: calculated to inform nomogram use and counseling; relative risk for bone metastasis or death increases as PSADT shortens
Testosterone levels: document castrate levels (<50 ng/dL) if progression occurs on ADT; monitor testosterone 12 weeks after first dose of LHRH therapy then upon increase in PSA
Serum calcium and creatinine: required prior to denosumab and zoledronic acid therapy; periodic monitoring with denosumab use
Vitamin D levels: recommended baseline assessment and monitoring during antiresorptive therapy
Liver function: monthly monitoring during abiraterone therapy
Potassium and phosphate: monthly monitoring during abiraterone therapy
Complete blood count: regular monitoring during chemotherapy and PARP inhibitor therapy
Hepatic and renal function: monitoring during olaparib and rucaparib therapy with dose adjustments as needed
supportive_follow_up: DEXA scan before starting ADT and every 1-2 years while on ADT or antiresorptive therapy
Fracture risk assessment using FRAX annually for all patients on ADT or remaining hypogonadal
Cardiovascular risk assessment and management of modifiable risk factors
Geriatric assessment consideration for older adults
NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List utilization including social determinants of health
Patient-reported outcomes assessment using validated instruments (e.g., EPIC-26)
Comprehensive dental evaluation before initiating osteoclast inhibitor therapy
Baseline DEXA and follow-up DEXA after 1 year of antiresorptive therapy
Consideration of intermittent ADT to reduce toxicity when appropriate

special_situations.key_message: Active surveillance is the preferred management approach for patients with low-risk prostate cancer and a life expectancy ≥10 years, with approximately 50% of eligible patients safely avoiding treatment for at least 10 years [272,275,278-281].
special_situations.management: Involves PSA monitoring (every 6 months), DRE (every 12 months), serial prostate biopsies (every 1-3 years), and serial mpMRI (every 12 months). Confirmatory testing including mpMRI with PSA density calculation and repeat biopsy within 1-2 years is required. Treatment is initiated upon grade reclassification to Grade Group ≥2 or other progression criteria. Factors associated with higher risk of near-term reclassification include high PSA density (≥0.15), high number of positive cores (≥3), higher BMI, faster PSA kinetics, and high genomic risk.
special_situations.evidence: Large cohort studies demonstrate 10-year metastasis rates <1% and 15-year cancer-specific survival rates of 94-99% [272,275,278-281,283]. The ProtecT trial showed no significant difference in prostate cancer mortality at 10-15 years between monitoring, surgery, or RT, though patients with intermediate-risk disease had higher rates of metastases in the monitoring arm [261,263].

special_situations.situation: Active Surveillance in Favorable Intermediate-Risk Disease
special_situations.key_message: A subset of patients with favorable intermediate-risk prostate cancer and life expectancy >10 years may be considered for active surveillance, particularly those with Grade Group 1 disease, but selection requires careful individualized assessment.
special_situations.management: Consideration for active surveillance should account for low percentage of Gleason pattern 4, low tumor volume, low PSA density, and/or low genomic risk. Active surveillance is not recommended for patients with unfavorable histology (expansile/large cribriform histology, intraductal carcinoma). Patients must understand that significant proportion of those staged as favorable intermediate-risk may have higher risk disease. Close monitoring for progression is essential.
special_situations.evidence: The Toronto group demonstrated inferior metastasis-free survival for intermediate-risk patients on active surveillance, with 15-year metastasis-free survival of 94% for Gleason 6 with PSA <10 ng/mL but only 63% for Gleason 4+3 with PSA ≤20 ng/mL [288]. Canary PASS showed 15-year risk of distant metastasis of approximately 10% for Grade Group 2 disease [284,289].

special_situations.key_message: For patients with high-risk BCR2 (PSADT ≤9 months, PSA ≥2 ng/mL above nadir after RT or ≥1 ng/mL after RP), treatment intensification with enzalutamide ± leuprolide or apalutamide + ADT can be considered based on EMBARK and PRESTO trials.
special_situations.management: Emboldened by the EMBARK trial, enzalutamide with or without leuprolide is an option for patients with high-risk criteria: M0 by CT, MRI, or bone scan; PSADT ≤9 months; PSA ≥2 ng/mL above nadir after RT or ≥1 ng/mL after RP; not candidate for pelvic-directed therapy. Apalutamide + ADT is a category 2B option for patients with BCR after RP meeting high-risk criteria: PSADT ≤9 months; PSA ≥0.5 ng/mL; prior adjuvant or secondary RT or not RT candidate.
special_situations.evidence: EMBARK: enzalutamide + leuprolide significantly improved metastasis-free survival vs leuprolide alone (HR, 0.42; P < .001); enzalutamide alone also improved MFS (HR, 0.63; P = .005) [478]. PRESTO: ADT + apalutamide improved PSA-PFS vs ADT alone (HR, 0.52; P = .00047) [479].

special_situations.key_message: MDT with SBRT to oligometastatic sites improves PFS and delays initiation of systemic therapy in metachronous oligorecurrent CSPC, based on multiple phase 2 randomized trials.
special_situations.management: MDT is recommended for patients with ≤5 metastatic sites by CT/MRI/bone scan or ≤10 by PSMA-PET. Should be considered for metachronous oligorecurrent disease in conjunction with or instead of ADT. For synchronous oligometastatic disease, concurrent MDT with recommended systemic therapy can be considered in select patients.
special_situations.evidence: ORIOLE: MDRT improved PFS vs observation (not reached vs 5.8 months; HR, 0.30; P = .002) [570]. STOMP: MDT improved ADT-free survival [569]. EXTEND: MDT + intermittent ADT improved PFS vs ADT alone (not reached vs 15.8 months; HR, 0.25; P < .001) [568].

special_situations.situation: PARP Inhibitor Therapy in BRCAm mCRPC
special_situations.key_message: Patients with BRCA1/2 mutations have pronounced benefit from PARP inhibitor monotherapy or PARP inhibitor/ARPI combinations, representing a paradigm of precision medicine in prostate cancer.
special_situations.management: Multiple PARP inhibitor options available based on prior therapy: olaparib (category 1), rucaparib (category 1, preferred pre-docetaxel), or combinations with ARPIs (olaparib/abiraterone, talazoparib/enzalutamide, niraparib/abiraterone). Metastatic biopsy strongly recommended; ctDNA assay option if biopsy unsafe/feasible. Germline testing recommended for BRCA1/2 mutations with implications for family cancer risk.
special_situations.evidence: PROfound: olaparib improved rPFS (HR, 0.34) and OS (HR, 0.69) vs abiraterone/enzalutamide in BRCA1/2/ATM cohort [652,653]. TRITON3: rucaparib improved PFS vs physician's choice in BRCAm patients (HR, 0.50) [656]. TALAPRO-2: talazoparib/enzalutamide improved rPFS (HR, 0.46 in HRRm) and OS (HR, 0.62 in HRRm cohort) [664,665].

special_situations.key_message: Lu-177-PSMA-617 is a category 1 radiopharmaceutical option for PSMA-positive mCRPC previously treated with ARPI and taxane, or who are appropriate candidates to delay taxane-based chemotherapy.
special_situations.management: Requires ≥1 PSMA-positive lesion and no dominant PSMA-negative metastatic lesions. PSMA-negative lesions defined as: bone with soft tissue components ≥1.0 cm, lymph nodes ≥2.5 cm in short axis, solid organ metastases ≥1.0 cm. Typically administered IV 200 mCi (7.4 GBq) every 6 weeks for up to 6 treatments. PSMA-PET imaging should only be used in M1 CRPC to determine candidacy.
special_situations.evidence: VISION: Lu-177-PSMA-617 improved OS (15.3 vs 11.3 months; HR, 0.62; P < .001) and PFS (8.7 vs 3.4 months; HR, 0.40; P < .001) [668]. PSMAfore: improved rPFS pre-docetaxel (11.6 vs 5.6 months; HR, 0.49; P < .001) [669].

special_situations.key_message: Small cell/neuroendocrine prostate cancer (NEPC) should be considered in patients with disease that no longer responds to ADT and who test positive for metastases, particularly those with low PSA levels despite large metastatic burden and visceral disease.
special_situations.management: Biopsy of accessible metastatic lesions is recommended to identify histomorphologic features. Cytotoxic chemotherapy options include cisplatin/etoposide, carboplatin/etoposide, docetaxel/carboplatin, cabazitaxel/carboplatin. Consult NCCN Guidelines for Small Cell Lung Cancer for additional options. DNA repair mutations and small cell/neuroendocrine histology are almost mutually exclusive.
special_situations.evidence: Multi-institutional prospective series found small cell/neuroendocrine histology in 17% of patients with mCRPC. Patients with this histology and prior ARPI exposure had shorter OS (HR, 2.02; P = .036) compared with adenocarcinoma [680].

special_situations.key_message: Individuals with prostate cancer are at increased risk for cardiovascular disease, primarily due to effects of ADT and ARPIs. CVD is a leading cause of death in this population.
special_situations.management: Risk assessment using ABCDE framework: Awareness of risks, Blood pressure management, Cholesterol assessment and smoking cessation, Diet and weight management, Exercise and echocardiogram/ECG based on individual risk. Aggressive risk factor management recommended. Referral to cardio-oncologist for patients at elevated CVD risk. Lifestyle modifications should be strongly promoted.
special_situations.evidence: ADT associated with increased risk for new diabetes (HR, 1.44), coronary artery disease (HR, 1.16), and myocardial infarction (HR, 1.11) [534]. ARPIs also associated with cardiovascular events [535,536].

special_situations.key_message: Triplet therapy with ADT, docetaxel, and an ARPI (abiraterone or darolutamide) is recommended for fit patients with high-volume mCSPC, based on PEACE-1 and ARASENS trials.
special_situations.management: ADT + docetaxel + abiraterone (PEACE-1) or ADT + docetaxel + darolutamide (ARASENS). Can also be combined with EBRT to primary tumor in select patients. The triplet approach is preferred for high-volume disease patients fit for chemotherapy. Doublet therapy (ADT + ARPI) remains a suitable alternative as no studies have directly compared doublet to triplet therapy.
special_situations.evidence: PEACE-1: abiraterone added to ADT + docetaxel improved rPFS (HR, 0.50; P < .0001) and OS (HR, 0.75; P = .017) [556]. ARASENS: darolutamide improved OS (HR, 0.68; P < .001), time to CRPC (HR, 0.36), skeletal event-free survival (HR, 0.61) [557].

special_situations.key_message: Significant disparities exist in prostate cancer incidence, stage at diagnosis, and mortality across racial and ethnic groups, driven primarily by differences in screening, access to care, and social determinants of health.
special_situations.management: Screening strategies should be adapted to individual risk, with awareness of higher risk in Black individuals (1 in 6 lifetime risk). Treatment disparities should be actively addressed. Patient navigation, genetic counseling, and attention to social determinants of health are essential. Outcomes are similar across racial groups when patients receive guideline-concordant care.
special_situations.evidence: Incidence in Black individuals is 67% higher than white individuals; mortality is two to four times higher [11]. Hispanic individuals more likely to present with higher risk disease and less likely to receive treatment [103,104]. Asian American individuals more likely diagnosed at higher risk group [105].

clinical_pearls: Approximately 50% of patients eligible for active surveillance may safely avoid treatment for at least 10 years, but approximately 70% will require treatment by 15 years, highlighting the importance of long-term follow-up compliance.

The 22-gene genomic classifier (Decipher) has demonstrated independent prognostic value for distant metastasis, prostate cancer-specific mortality, and overall survival across multiple phase 3 randomized trials, and should be used when it has the potential to change management.

ADT monotherapy should not be used as primary treatment for clinically localized prostate cancer unless there is a clear contraindication to definitive local therapy; combination therapy is strongly recommended for metastatic castration-sensitive disease.

PSMA-PET/CT has superior sensitivity and specificity compared to conventional imaging for detecting micrometastatic disease, but expert interpretation is essential to avoid false-positive classification that could lead to inappropriate treatment escalation.

Intermittent ADT can be considered to reduce toxicity in appropriate patients with metastatic disease, particularly those with M0 recurrent disease, though close monitoring during off-treatment periods is essential.

For patients with BRCA2-mutated metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer, niraparib/abiraterone is a new option, but benefit in low-volume disease is controversial due to limited data in this population.

Biopsy of accessible metastatic lesions should be performed in all patients with mCRPC to identify small cell/neuroendocrine histology and perform molecular testing, as this fundamentally changes the treatment approach.

Black individuals with prostate cancer have 67% higher incidence and 2-4x higher mortality rates than other racial/ethnic groups, but outcomes are equivalent when receiving guideline-concordant care, underscoring the critical importance of equitable treatment access.

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology: Prostate Cancer
guidelines_resources.notes: Version 5.2026; comprehensive multidisciplinary guideline covering all disease states from localized to metastatic; updated January 23, 2026; available at www.nccn.org

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Prostate Cancer Early Detection
guidelines_resources.notes: Addresses PSA-based screening and early detection strategies; complementary to the treatment guideline; available at www.nccn.org

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Genetic/Familial High-Risk Assessment: Breast, Ovarian, Pancreatic, and Prostate
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides criteria for germline genetic testing in prostate cancer patients; critical for identifying BRCA2, ATM, and other HRR mutations with treatment implications

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship
guidelines_resources.notes: Addresses survivorship issues including bone health, cardiovascular disease, psychosocial support, and long-term monitoring for prostate cancer survivors

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Hematopoietic Growth Factors
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides recommendations for G-CSF support during chemotherapy; relevant for patients receiving docetaxel or cabazitaxel

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Distress Management
guidelines_resources.notes: Includes NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List with social determinants of health; recommended for use in prostate cancer management

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Older Adult Oncology
guidelines_resources.notes: Tools for optimal assessment and management of disease in older adults; includes life expectancy estimation guidance

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Small Cell Lung Cancer
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for treatment of small cell/neuroendocrine prostate cancer due to similar behavior to small cell lung cancer

guidelines_resources.title: NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immunotherapy-Related Toxicities
guidelines_resources.notes: Addresses immune-mediated adverse reactions from pembrolizumab and other immune checkpoint inhibitors

guidelines_resources.title: The Clinician's Guide to Prevention and Treatment of Osteoporosis
guidelines_resources.notes: Referenced for bone health screening and treatment recommendations during ADT [Ref 1]

guidelines_resources.title: ISCD Official Positions
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides recommendations for DEXA monitoring frequency during antiresorptive therapy

guidelines_resources.title: ACR Appropriateness Criteria
guidelines_resources.notes: Provides appropriateness criteria for imaging use in prostate cancer

guidelines_resources.title: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, Eighth Edition
guidelines_resources.notes: Defines TNM staging system used in the NCCN guidelines; includes prognostic group definitions and Grade Group system

key_trials.full_name: Abiraterone acetate plus prednisone in metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer
key_trials.population: High-risk mCSPC (≥2 of: Gleason score 8-10, ≥3 bone metastases, visceral metastases)
key_trials.key_result: OS significantly improved (HR, 0.62; P < .0001). Final OS: median 53.3 vs 36.5 months (HR, 0.66; P < .0001)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Delayed castration-resistant progression (14.8 vs 33.2 months), PSA progression, time to pain progression, chemotherapy initiation all improved
key_trials.practice_change: Established abiraterone as standard component of first-line therapy for high-risk mCSPC

key_trials.full_name: Systemic Therapy in Advancing or Metastatic Prostate Cancer: Evaluation of Drug Efficacy
key_trials.population: M1 CSPC, N1M0, and high-risk M0 disease
key_trials.key_result: OS improved (HR, 0.63; P < .0001) in overall population; benefit seen across M1, N1, and M0 subsets
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: FFS improved (HR, 0.29; P < .0001); benefit in survival larger in patients <70 years than ≥70 years
key_trials.practice_change: Established abiraterone as standard for newly diagnosed mCSPC and high-risk node-positive/non-metastatic disease

key_trials.full_name: Apalutamide for metastatic, castration-sensitive prostate cancer
key_trials.key_result: rPFS: HR, 0.48 (P < .001); OS: HR, 0.65 (P < .001) at final analysis with median follow-up 44 months
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Time to CRPC, time to pain progression, time to chemotherapy all delayed; QOL maintained
key_trials.practice_change: Established apalutamide as category 1 option for mCSPC

key_trials.full_name: Enzalutamide with standard first-line therapy in metastatic prostate cancer
key_trials.key_result: OS: HR, 0.67 (P = .002) at 34-month follow-up; HR, 0.70 at 68-month follow-up (P < .0001)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: PFS using PSA levels and clinical PFS improved
key_trials.practice_change: Established enzalutamide as category 1 option for mCSPC

key_trials.full_name: Randomized phase III study of androgen deprivation therapy with enzalutamide or placebo in men with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer
key_trials.key_result: rPFS: HR, 0.39 (P < .001); OS: HR, 0.66 (P < .001); ~32% of placebo patients crossed over to enzalutamide
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Time to PSA progression, time to new antineoplastic therapy, time to deterioration of QOL all improved
key_trials.practice_change: Confirmed enzalutamide benefit in mCSPC

key_trials.full_name: Abiraterone acetate and prednisone added to androgen deprivation therapy and docetaxel in de novo metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer
key_trials.key_result: rPFS improved with abiraterone (HR, 0.54; P < .0001); OS improved (HR, 0.82; P = .030) in docetaxel-receiving patients
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Time to CRPC, time to pain progression delayed; RT to primary tumor benefits only seen with abiraterone
key_trials.practice_change: Established triplet therapy (ADT + docetaxel + abiraterone) as category 1 option for high-volume mCSPC

key_trials.full_name: Darolutamide plus androgen-deprivation therapy and docetaxel in metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Time to CRPC (HR, 0.36), skeletal event-free survival (HR, 0.61), time to subsequent therapy (HR, 0.39) all significantly improved
key_trials.practice_change: Established triplet therapy with darolutamide as category 1 option for high-volume mCSPC

key_trials.full_name: Enzalutamide in men with nonmetastatic, castration-resistant prostate cancer
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Delayed pain progression, symptom worsening, functional status decrease
key_trials.practice_change: Established enzalutamide for M0 CRPC with PSADT ≤10 months

key_trials.full_name: Apalutamide treatment and metastasis-free survival in prostate cancer
key_trials.key_result: MFS: 40.5 vs 16.2 months (HR, 0.28; P < .001); final OS: 73.9 vs 59.9 months (HR, 0.78; P = .016)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: QOL maintained; adverse events include rash, fracture, hypothyroidism
key_trials.practice_change: Established apalutamide for M0 CRPC with PSADT ≤10 months

key_trials.full_name: Darolutamide in nonmetastatic, castration-resistant prostate cancer
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Fatigue, pain in extremity, rash more common; fracture rates similar between arms
key_trials.practice_change: Established darolutamide for M0 CRPC with PSADT ≤10 months

key_trials.full_name: Abiraterone and prednisone vs placebo in patients with mCRPC post-docetaxel
key_trials.key_result: Median OS: 15.8 vs 11.2 months (HR, 0.74; P < .0001)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Time to radiographic progression, PSA decline, pain palliation all improved
key_trials.practice_change: First FDA approval of abiraterone for mCRPC

key_trials.full_name: Abiraterone in metastatic prostate cancer without previous chemotherapy
key_trials.population: Asymptomatic or minimally symptomatic mCRPC, no prior chemotherapy
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Time to symptomatic deterioration, time to chemotherapy, time to pain progression, PSA responses all improved
key_trials.practice_change: Extended abiraterone use to pre-docetaxel mCRPC

key_trials.full_name: Increased survival with enzalutamide in prostate cancer after chemotherapy
key_trials.key_result: Median OS: 18.4 vs 13.6 months (HR, 0.63; P < .001)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: PSA decline >50% (54% vs 2%), radiographic response (29% vs 4%), time to first SRE (16.7 vs 13.3 months) improved
key_trials.practice_change: First FDA approval of enzalutamide for mCRPC

key_trials.full_name: Enzalutamide in metastatic prostate cancer before chemotherapy
key_trials.key_result: rPFS: 20.0 vs 5.4 months; OS: 35.3 vs 31.3 months
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Time to chemotherapy, time to first SRE, PSA responses all improved
key_trials.practice_change: Extended enzalutamide to pre-chemotherapy mCRPC

key_trials.full_name: Olaparib for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
key_trials.population: mCRPC with HRR gene mutations, prior abiraterone or enzalutamide
key_trials.key_result: rPFS cohort A (BRCA1/2/ATM): HR, 0.34 (P < .001); entire study: HR, 0.49 (P < .001). OS cohort A: HR, 0.69 (P = .02)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Time to pain progression, time to next therapy, objective response rate improved
key_trials.practice_change: Established olaparib as category 1 for BRCAm mCRPC

key_trials.full_name: Rucaparib or physician's choice in metastatic prostate cancer
key_trials.population: mCRPC with BRCA1/2 or ATM mutation, prior ARPI, no prior chemotherapy for mCRPC
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS: 10.2 vs 6.4 months (HR, 0.61; P < .001); BRCAm subgroup: 11.2 vs 6.4 months (HR, 0.50)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Objective response rate in BRCAm population; fatigue and nausea most common adverse events
key_trials.practice_change: Established rucaparib as category 1 for BRCAm mCRPC post-ARPI

key_trials.full_name: Talazoparib plus enzalutamide in men with first-line metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Time to symptomatic progression, time to chemotherapy; hematologic adverse events more frequent in talazoparib arm
key_trials.practice_change: Established talazoparib/enzalutamide for HRRm mCRPC

key_trials.full_name: Abiraterone and olaparib for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
key_trials.population: mCRPC regardless of HRR mutation status
key_trials.key_result: rPFS: 24.8 vs 16.6 months (HR, 0.66; P < .001); HRRm: HR, 0.50. Final OS: HR, 0.81 (P = .054, not significant)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: PSA response, time to pain progression improved; anemia, fatigue, nausea most common
key_trials.practice_change: Established olaparib/abiraterone for BRCAm mCRPC

key_trials.full_name: Niraparib and abiraterone acetate for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
key_trials.population: mCRPC with HRR mutations, prior ARPI
key_trials.key_result: rPFS in HRRm: 16.5 vs 13.7 months (HR, 0.73; P = .022); BRCAm: 16.6 vs 10.9 months (HR, 0.53; P = .001)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Time to symptomatic progression, time to chemotherapy improved; OS analysis favorable (HR, 0.54)
key_trials.practice_change: Established niraparib/abiraterone for BRCAm mCRPC

key_trials.full_name: Lutetium-177-PSMA-617 for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
key_trials.population: PSMA-positive mCRPC, prior ARPI and taxane
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: PSA response, quality of life; anemia, thrombocytopenia, lymphopenia more common
key_trials.practice_change: Established Lu-177-PSMA-617 for PSMA-positive mCRPC post-ARPI and taxane

key_trials.full_name: Lu-177-PSMA-617 versus change of ARPI therapy for taxane-naive mCRPC
key_trials.key_result: rPFS: 11.6 vs 5.6 months (HR, 0.49; P < .001); OS not significantly different (crossover 57%)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Fewer grade 3-5 toxicities with Lu-177-PSMA-617
key_trials.practice_change: Expanded Lu-177-PSMA-617 indication to pre-taxane mCRPC

key_trials.full_name: Alpha emitter radium-223 and survival in metastatic prostate cancer
key_trials.population: Symptomatic mCRPC with bone metastases, no visceral metastases
key_trials.key_result: Median OS: 14.9 vs 11.3 months (HR, 0.70; P < .001); time to first SRE: 15.6 vs 9.8 months
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Low grade 3-4 hematologic toxicity; improved QOL decline
key_trials.practice_change: Established radium-223 for symptomatic bone-metastatic mCRPC without visceral metastases

key_trials.full_name: Enzalutamide plus radium-223 in metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
key_trials.key_result: rPFS: HR, 0.69 (P = .0009); interim OS: HR, 0.69 (P = .0031); bone-protecting agents mandatory
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Grade ≥3 AEs more common (65.6% vs 55.8%); fractures 24.3% vs 13.4% but mitigated with bone agents
key_trials.practice_change: Supports combination of radium-223 + enzalutamide with mandatory bone-protecting agents

key_trials.full_name: Cabazitaxel versus abiraterone or enzalutamide in metastatic prostate cancer
key_trials.population: mCRPC post-docetaxel and either abiraterone or enzalutamide
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Pain response, time to pain progression, time to SREs all improved with cabazitaxel
key_trials.practice_change: Demonstrated cabazitaxel superior to alternate ARPI after docetaxel and ARPI; supports sequential use of distinct mechanisms

key_trials.full_name: Chemohormonal therapy in metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer
key_trials.key_result: OS: 57.6 vs 47.2 months (HR, 0.72; P = .002); benefit most pronounced in high-volume disease
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: High-volume subgroup: HR, 0.60; low-volume subgroup: HR, 1.04 (no benefit)
key_trials.practice_change: Established docetaxel as component of upfront therapy for high-volume mCSPC

key_trials.full_name: Docetaxel plus prednisone or mitoxantrone plus prednisone for advanced prostate cancer
key_trials.key_result: Median OS: 18.9 vs 16.5 months (P = .009)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Pain response, PSA decline, quality of life improved
key_trials.practice_change: First chemotherapy to demonstrate survival benefit in mCRPC; established docetaxel as standard

key_trials.full_name: Prednisone plus cabazitaxel or mitoxantrone for metastatic castration-resistant prostate cancer
key_trials.key_result: OS: HR, 0.72 (P < .0001); 2.4-month survival improvement
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Febrile neutropenia 7.5%, severe diarrhea 6%, anemia 11%
key_trials.practice_change: Established cabazitaxel for post-docetaxel mCRPC

key_trials.full_name: Sipuleucel-T immunotherapy for castration-resistant prostate cancer
key_trials.key_result: Median OS: 25.8 vs 21.7 months (HR, 0.78; P = .03); 22% reduction in mortality risk
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Mild to moderate chills 54.1%, pyrexia 29.3%, headache 16.0%
key_trials.practice_change: First cancer immunotherapy to demonstrate survival benefit; established for asymptomatic mCRPC

key_trials.full_name: Improved outcomes with enzalutamide in biochemically recurrent prostate cancer
key_trials.population: High-risk BCR (M0 by CT/MRI/bone scan; PSADT ≤9 months)
key_trials.key_result: Enzalutamide + leuprolide: HR, 0.42 (P < .001); enzalutamide alone: HR, 0.63 (P = .005) vs leuprolide alone
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Gynecomastia 45% with enzalutamide monotherapy; nipple pain 15%; breast tenderness 14%
key_trials.practice_change: Established enzalutamide ± leuprolide for high-risk BCR

key_trials.full_name: Intensification of androgen blockade in patients with high-risk biochemically relapsed castration-sensitive prostate cancer
key_trials.population: High-risk BCR after RP (PSADT ≤9 months; PSA ≥0.5 ng/mL; prior RT or not RT candidate)
key_trials.key_result: ADT + apalutamide: HR, 0.52 (P = .00047); ADT + apalutamide + AAP: HR, 0.48 (P = .00008) vs ADT alone
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: No significant further benefit adding AAP to apalutamide; hypertension most common grade ≥3 AE
key_trials.practice_change: Established apalutamide + ADT as category 2B option for high-risk BCR after maximal pelvic therapy

key_trials.full_name: Niraparib and abiraterone acetate plus prednisone for HRR-deficient metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer
key_trials.key_result: rPFS in BRCAm: not reached vs 26.0 months (HR, 0.52; P < .0001); full ITT: HR, 0.63 (P = 0.0001)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Grade 3/4 AEs: 75% vs 59%; treatment-related deaths: 14 vs 7; anemia 29.1% vs 4.6%
key_trials.practice_change: Established niraparib/abiraterone as option for BRCA2m mCSPC

key_trials.full_name: Outcomes of observation vs stereotactic ablative radiation for oligometastatic prostate cancer
key_trials.population: Previously treated, 1-3 metastases by conventional imaging
key_trials.key_result: Median PFS: not reached vs 5.8 months (HR, 0.30; P = .002)
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Treatment well tolerated; combined analysis with STOMP demonstrated pooled HR, 0.44 (P < .001)
key_trials.practice_change: Supports MDT for metachronous oligorecurrent CSPC

key_trials.full_name: Stereotactic Ablative Radiotherapy for the Comprehensive Treatment of Oligometastatic Cancers
key_trials.population: Oligometastatic (1-5 metastases) breast, lung, colorectal, prostate cancers
key_trials.key_result: 5-year OS: 42.3% vs 17.7% (P = .006) in total population
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Including 16 prostate cancer patients (14 randomized to MDRT); post-hoc analysis excluding prostate patients showed trend in favor of MDRT
key_trials.practice_change: Supports MDT approach in oligometastatic disease

key_trials.full_name: Androgen suppression combined with elective nodal and dose escalated radiation therapy
key_trials.journal: Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys
key_trials.intervention: EBRT + LDR brachytherapy boost + 12 months ADT
key_trials.comparator: EBRT boost to 78 Gy + 12 months ADT
key_trials.key_result: bPFS: 86% vs 75% at 7 years (P < .001); grade 3 GU toxicity: 18.4% vs 5.2%
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Higher toxicity with brachytherapy boost; no OS benefit demonstrated
key_trials.practice_change: Supports brachytherapy boost for dose escalation with careful patient selection

key_trials.full_name: Ultrahypofractionated versus conventionally fractionated radiotherapy for prostate cancer
key_trials.key_result: Noninferior for FFS; no significant difference in toxicity
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Patient convenience and reduced treatment burden
key_trials.practice_change: Established ultra-hypofractionation as noninferior to conventional fractionation

key_trials.full_name: Phase 3 trial of stereotactic body radiotherapy in localized prostate cancer
key_trials.key_result: Noninferior for tumor control; generally similar safety profiles
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Patient convenience; SBRT requires precision treatment setup
key_trials.practice_change: Established SBRT as noninferior to moderate hypofractionation for low- and intermediate-risk disease

key_trials.full_name: Timing of radiotherapy after radical prostatectomy
key_trials.comparator: Monitoring with policy to treat at PSA 0.1 ng/mL or rising
key_trials.key_result: No difference in 5-year bPFS; no difference in OS; urinary incontinence and urethral strictures more frequent with adjuvant
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Freedom from distant metastasis at 10 years: 93% vs 90% (P = .095)
key_trials.practice_change: Supports early secondary RT over immediate adjuvant RT for most patients

key_trials.full_name: Prostate testing for cancer and treatment
key_trials.intervention: Active monitoring, radical prostatectomy, or radiotherapy
key_trials.key_result: No significant difference in prostate cancer mortality at 10-15 years (P = .48); 12.2% increase in disease progression and 3.4% increase in metastases/death in active monitoring group
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Surgery associated with greater urinary incontinence and impotence; RT associated with slight decrease in bowel function; 70% of monitoring arm received treatment by 15 years
key_trials.practice_change: Demonstrated safety of conservative management for low-risk disease; highlighted importance of risk-adapted treatment selection
